   2 cost can never go below 0). The numerical results would barely
   change but the artifact would look tighter.

4. **The decision-framework table is a recommendation in spec form.** If

   separate "decisions" artifact, I can move that table.

Let me know which (if any) of those you want changed before I move this
addendum into spec.md.

Commission C is brief-draftable once C2–C5 land."

- session: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
  date: 2026-04-29
  item: "X010 H4 cost-model simulation. Conversation started open-ended on polyrepo (would splitting nexus help with commission cost/wall time?), and dug into two example rigs (rig-moji64hs vision-keeper cleanup, rig-moj12h4o reckoner tick) to characterize where the cost lives. Found implementer dominates cost (55-60% of rig spend) and is driven by cumulative cache-read replay across 130-150 turns × ~150-270K avg context. Derived a session-economics formula: total cost ≈ ∫context(t)dt; concave context curve makes cost super-linear in turn count (~40% above linear projection). Computed empirical break-even for naive splits — 30K-handoff midpoint splits save ~50% on 150-turn sessions vs ~10% with full re-read. Sean noted the prior X010 H1 piece-session experiment found splitting was way more expensive when each split re-orientation was forced. Built simulation script (h4a_naive_split.py + h4a_summary.py) testing both naive-split and handoff-split scenarios across 101 archived transcripts ≥ 50 turns plus the 2 recent rigs. Findings: naive split rarely pays off (73% of sessions never break even at any split point; empirical break-even at ~50-80 post-split turns, not 30 as the analytical formula suggested); 30K-handoff splits work in 93% of sessions with median 33% savings; the gap between naive and handoff is 30-95 percentage points. Wrote artifact 2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md formalizing the cost model and presenting cohort statistics. Drafted spec addendum in .scratch/x010-h4-addendum.md proposing H4a (naive break-even formula), H4b (handoff dominates), H4c (orientation-tax suppression as success criterion for c-modxxtu6) as sharpened sub-hypotheses with falsifiable thresholds. Spec edit pending Sean's review of the draft."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

input.command:
GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local \
  git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
X010: simulate naive vs handoff session splits across the transcript archive

Adds a new artifact (2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md) and two
analysis scripts (h4a_naive_split.py, h4a_summary.py) modeling the cost
of split-session strategies against per-turn cache-read curves from 101
archived transcripts plus 2 recent rig transcripts.

Findings:
- Naive splits (no handoff, full re-read) rarely pay off: 73% of sessions
  have no split point with positive savings. Empirical break-even is ~50-80
  turns of post-split work, higher than the analytical formula's 30.
- 30K-handoff midpoint splits save 33% median savings across 93% of
  sessions; long sessions (300+ turns) save 50%+.
- Confirms the X010 H1 piece-session result was structurally inevitable —
  per-task pieces averaged 30-44 turns, well below the naive break-even.

The artifact tees up sharpened H4a/H4b/H4c sub-hypotheses with falsifiable
thresholds and quantitative targets for the c-modxxtu6 checkpoint
architecture (handoff <30K, turn-count expansion <1.3x). Spec addendum
draft is in .scratch/x010-h4-addendum.md awaiting Sean's review before
landing in spec.md.

Session: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -10

content.text:
Done. Here's where things stand:

## What landed

**Committed (`265333e6`)**:
- `experiments/X010-staged-sessions/artifacts/2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md` — the empirical findings artifact
- `experiments/X010-staged-sessions/artifacts/scripts/h4a_naive_split.py` — the simulation engine
- `experiments/X010-staged-sessions/artifacts/scripts/h4a_summary.py` — cohort aggregation across the archive
- `experiments/data/coco-log.yaml` — log entry

**Pending your review at** `.scratch/x010-h4-addendum.md`:
- Draft addendum formalizing H4a/H4b/H4c as sub-hypotheses with thresholds, plus a decision-framework table by session length
- I included a "Notes for Sean" section at the end flagging four things I'd like your call on before the addendum lands in `spec.md`

## Headline findings from the simulation (101 transcripts ≥50 turns)

- **Naive splits rarely pay off.** 73% of sessions have *no* split point that saves money under naive splitting (full re-read). Empirical break-even is ~50–80 turns of post-split work — meaningfully higher than the ~30-turn analytical prediction I gave you earlier. The formula was directionally right but under-counted baseline cost.
- **30K handoff splits work almost universally.** 93% of sessions show positive savings at midpoint; median savings is 33%. Long sessions (300+ turns) median 50%+.
- **The previous X010 H1 piece-session experiment was structurally guaranteed to fail.** Pieces averaged 30–44 turns each — below the naive break-even — *and* incurred per-piece orientation tax that expanded total turns 3.5×. Both effects compound. The cost model fully explains the 2.6× cost premium that was observed.

## Open questions for you on the addendum draft

In the "Notes for Sean" section at the bottom of `.scratch/x010-h4-addendum.md`, I flagged four:

1. Whether to keep H4a as a full sub-hypothesis or fold it into a footnote on H4c (it's somewhat academic since naive splitting essentially never works in our regime)
2. Whether to back H4c with a real per-piece-orientation measurement from the original X010 H1 piece-session transcripts (currently the evidence is the directional 3.5× turn-expansion observation)
3. Whether to clamp the savings-can't-exceed-100% caveat in the model (currently has a "model artifacts" caveat for cleanliness)
4. Whether the decision-framework table belongs in `spec.md` or in a separate decisions artifact

Let me know which you'd like changed before I move the addendum into `spec.md`. Or if you'd rather inspect the artifact and code first and circle back to the addendum later, that's fine too — the published artifact stands on its own and the addendum draft will sit happily in `.scratch/`.

content.text: Whoa. Look at the asymmetry between the two rigs.

content.content:
### Tick handler — fixed sequence per fire
  
  On each fire of the `reckoner.tick` relay:
  
  1. Resolve the active scheduler from the registry (cached at start by
     the scheduler-registry commission).
  2. Re-read `reckoner.schedulerConfig` from `guild.json`. Validate via
     the resolved scheduler's `validateConfig` if present. Throw → log
     fail-loud, skip this tick.
  3. Query candidates: held petitions are writs in their initial-equivalent
     phase carrying `ext.reckoner`. Use the same query shape today's
     `runCatchUpScan` uses (find-by-phase, then filter to those with
     `ext.reckoner`).
  4. Apply the existing source-validation gate (`isSourceRegistered` against
     `enforceRegistration`) and disabled-source filter (`isSourceDisabled`).
     Writs failing those gates produce a Reckonings decline row and skip
     the scheduler call.
  5. Build `SchedulerInput { candidates, capacity, now, config }`.
     `capacity` is the v0 stub from the registry commission. `now` is
     `new Date()`.
  6. Call `scheduler.evaluate(input)`. Apply each returned `SchedulerDecision`:
     - `approve` → `clerk.transition` to the writ's active target phase
       (reuse the existing `resolveActiveTargetPhase` helper).
     - `decline` → `clerk.transition` to the writ's cancelled target phase.
     - `defer` → no transition; outcome recorded as a Reckonings row only.
  7. Append one Reckonings row per writ considered, mirroring the existing
     per-action shape.
  
  The Reckoner's existing per-action idempotency check (`writId` plus
  the writ's current `updatedAt` as the dedupe key) carries forward — a
  writ already processed at its current `updatedAt` is a no-op for the
  tick. This keeps repeated ticks against unchanged writs from emitting
  duplicate Reckonings rows.
  
  Source: c-moiw5wkv (D-6, D-7), c-moisx6fx (per-call config flow).
  
  ### Standing order — kit-contributed via the new substrate
  
  The Reckoner's kit contribution gains a standing-order entry alongside
  its existing `relays` / `events` contributions:
  
      { schedule: '@every 60s', run: 'reckoner.tick' }
  
  No `id` field (per the additive-merge model from the Clockworks
  kit-contributed standing-orders commission). Operators may append
  additional standing orders in `guild.json` if they want them, but they
  cannot override or disable this one in this commission.
  
  Source: c-moiw5wkv (D-5); the additive-merge model from the Clockworks
  kit-standing-orders commission.
  
  ### Behavioral cases that must hold
  
  - A guild with no held petitions ticks every 60s and writes nothing —
    no Reckonings rows, no errors. The empty-candidate path is exercised.
  - Pre-existing held petitions at apparatus start are picked up on the
    first tick after start, not at start itself. No catch-up scan runs.
  - A scheduler that throws inside `evaluate` does not corrupt apparatus
    state — the tick is skipped, no rows are written, the apparatus stays
    up.
  - A held petition whose source becomes disabled mid-flight gets a
    decline row on the next tick (the disabled-source gate runs every
    tick, not just at petition time).
  - Repeated ticks against the same writ at the same `updatedAt` are
    idempotent — no duplicate Reckonings rows.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - **Operator-configurable tick cadence.** Hard-coded `@every 60s` for
    this commission; future enhancement parked.
  - **Tick disable / pause mechanism.** No way to suspend the tick from
    operator config in this commission.
  - **Capacity-aware scheduling.** `CapacitySnapshot` remains a stub from
    the registry commission; capacity-tracking lands when a capacity-aware
    scheduler does.
  - **Multi-scheduler dispatch within a tick.** One active scheduler per
    Reckoner instance.
  - **Pulse / framework-event emissions on tick.** The auto-wired Clockworks
    book events on `reckoner/reckonings` continue to fire as today; no new
    framework events from this commission.
  
  ## References
  
  - **c-mod99ris** — Reckoner design parent.
  - **c-moiw5lvp** — umbrella click (tick-registration via Option B).
  - **c-moiw5wkv** — design click for this commission (locked decisions
    D-5, D-6, D-7).
  - **c-moixb74x** — parked future-improvement: operator-configurable tick
    cadence.
  - **c-moisx6fx** — scheduler kit-contribution registry (this commission
    depends on the registry brief).

Links:
  → w-moix4pe8  (follows)
  → w-moiy7bmo  (follows)
  ← w-moj1pa5r  (depends on)
  ← w-moj1pa7s  (depends on)
  ← w-moiyh0jz  (depends on)
  ← w-moj1pa3g  (lifted from)

content.content:
{
  "id": "w-moiy8hkv-dfb884cac01b",
  "codex": "nexus",
  "status": "completed",
  "createdAt": "2026-04-28T19:37:18.653Z",
  "updatedAt": "2026-04-28T19:55:02.614Z",
  "inventory": "# Inventory — Periodic tick for the Reckoner

## Scope and blast radius

This commission is **internal to the Reckoner apparatus** plus a
soft (kit-contribution) edge with the Clockworks. No other apparatus
imports the Reckoner's CDC code path, so the removal blast radius is
narrowly contained inside `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/**`.

Two cross-cutting touches outside that tree:

1. **`@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus` kit surface** — the Reckoner
   becomes a new consumer of two existing kit types: `relays` (for
   the `reckoner.tick` handler) and `standingOrders` (for the
   `@every 60s` entry). Both are already-shipped substrates from the
   sibling commissions `w-moix4pe8` (kit-standing-orders) and the
   `relays` kit type that pre-dates them. The Reckoner contributes
   through its own `apparatus.supportKit` block — no Clockworks code
   changes required. Indirect dependency declaration: the Reckoner
   should add `recommends: ['clockworks']` to its apparatus descriptor
   so the dependency relation is visible to Arbor's topo sort and
   readers (concurrent doc updates needed in
   `docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md` Dependencies block).

2. **Behavioral change visible to operators / petitioners** — held
   petition latency moves from "approved on the next CDC update of the
   writ" (≈ms after `setWritExt` returns) to "approved on the next
   tick after the petition is in `new`" (≤60s). This is documented
   non-negotiable in the brief; only relevant here as a doc-touch in
   the apparatus README.

## Affected files (Reckoner package)

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/reckoner.ts` — the heart of the
  change. Currently 1416 lines:
  - **Removed sections**: the `stacks.watch<WritDoc>('clerk',
    'writs', …)` subscription at the bottom of `start()` (lines
    1357–1362); the `handleWritsChange` function (lines 1111–1130)
    including the re-firing gate (D14 in current code); the
    `runCatchUpScan` function (lines 1146–1169); the call to
    `runCatchUpScan()` from the `phase:started` handler (line 1349);
    test-hook surface entries `handleWritsChange` and `runCatchUpScan`
    on `ReckonerTestHooks` (interface lines 250–256, hook-object lines
    1375–1376).
  - **Modified sections**: `runScheduler` (lines 906–1104) — currently
    takes a single `(writ, ext, now)` triple and builds
    `candidates: [writ]`. The brief prescribes a batch shape — the tick
    builds one `SchedulerInput` for the whole candidate set per fire.
    `considerWrit` (lines 795–876) — the brief says "becomes the
    per-writ branch invoked from the tick loop or its logic folds into
    the tick handler directly — implementer's call" (D5 in this plan).
    `start()` body — the `phase:started` handler stops calling
    `runCatchUpScan`. `apparatus.supportKit` — gains a `relays:
    [reckonerTickRelay]` slot and a `standingOrders: [{ schedule:
    '@every 60s', run: 'reckoner.tick' }]` slot.
  - **Untouched but adjacent**: `resolveActiveTargetPhase` (the
    type-aware target-phase resolver) is reused verbatim by the tick's
    approve path. `alreadyConsidered` (the `(writId,
    writUpdatedAt)` dedupe lookup) is reused verbatim. `resolveConfig`
    / `resolveSchedulerConfig` / `resolveActiveScheduler` are all
    reused verbatim. `buildReckoningRow` is reused with one new
    optional param (`tickEventId`) when present.

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/tick.ts` — **new file** holding the
  `createReckonerTickRelay()` factory and the pure tick-evaluation
  helper. Mirrors the
  `packages/plugins/clockworks/src/summon-relay.ts` /
  `packages/plugins/vision-keeper/src/decline-relay.ts` pattern:
  pure `relay()` definition plus an exported handler-body helper that
  takes its dependencies (clerk, stacks, reckoningsBook, registry,
  activeScheduler, resolveSchedulerConfig, resolveConfig,
  resolveActiveTargetPhase) by injection so unit tests can drive it
  without Clockworks. The `buildReckoner()` closure threads its state
  into the relay factory at apparatus boot time. Anchored in
  reckoner.ts as long as it stays small (~150 lines); separating to a
  sibling file follows the established pattern.

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/reckoner-cdc.test.ts` (937 lines) —
  **must be replaced**. Every test in this file asserts CDC-driven
  behavior (`hooks.handleWritsChange`, `setWritExt` Phase 2 dispatch
  triggering accept paths, the re-firing gate at D14). With CDC gone,
  none of those entry points exist. The behavioral cases the file
  enumerates (skip-not-in-new, skip-no-ext, disabled-source skip,
  unregistered-strict decline, unregistered-non-strict approve,
  registered-source approve, ext-restamp re-evaluation, CDC re-delivery
  idempotency, startup catch-up, withdrawal-mid-flight, type-aware
  target-phase resolution) all need re-expressing through the tick
  path. Most still apply (phase gate, ext gate, registered-source
  approve, idempotency, withdrawal-mid-flight, type-aware target);
  three change semantics or disappear (disabled-source skip changes
  meaning per the brief — see decisions; ext-restamp re-evaluation gate
  is gone with CDC; "startup catch-up" becomes "first tick after start
  picks up pre-existing held writs"). Recommended layout: replace
  with `reckoner-tick.test.ts` covering the surviving / changed cases
  via a `hooks.runTick(syntheticTimerEvent)` entry point.

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/reckoner-scheduler.test.ts` (942
  lines) — **most tests survive** but the entry point shifts from
  `fix.reckoner.petition(...)` triggering CDC dispatch to
  `hooks.runTick(...)` invocations. Specific tests that drive the
  scheduler error paths (validateConfig throw, evaluate throw,
  multi-decision per writ id, stranger writId, approve / defer /
  decline outcome mapping, weight threading) all need the entry to
  switch from "petition then observe immediate dispatch" to "petition
  then runTick then observe outcome". Some assertions (e.g. the defer
  test currently asserts "no row, no transition") may need to flip
  if D17 (defer-writes-row) is selected.

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/integration.test.ts` (288 lines) —
  asserts the same end-to-end accept flow through the public
  `petition()` helper. The setWritExt Phase 2 dispatch path no longer
  drives the auto-approve; the test must either run a tick after the
  petition lands, or assert the writ stays in `new` until the tick
  fires. The two existing test bodies need updating; both are short.

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/index.ts` — no changes expected.
  The `Scheduler` / `SchedulerInput` / `SchedulerDecision` /
  `CapacitySnapshot` / `HeldWrit` types are already exported.

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/types.ts` — no changes expected.
  `ReckoningDoc.tickEventId` is already declared (line 308; with
  comment that says "always absent on v0 rows" — concurrent doc
  updates needed: the comment should now say "stamped from the
  triggering `clockworks.timer` event id when the consideration was
  triggered by a tick; always absent on CDC-driven considerations
  which v0 no longer ships").

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/reckoner.test.ts` (not opened) —
  scope holds the pre-existing kit-registry tests (petitioner-source
  validation, seal lifecycle, etc.). These do not exercise the CDC
  handler and should pass unchanged.

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/schedulers/always-approve.ts` — no
  changes. The default scheduler keeps its shape; the only difference
  is that `evaluate` will now be handed a multi-element `candidates`
  array per tick rather than the per-call single-element array.

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/package.json` — no changes expected
  (kit contributions don't require new dependency declarations; the
  `relays` and `standingOrders` kit types live on the existing
  `@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus` package which is already
  resolvable through the existing dep graph).

- `packages/plugins/reckoner/README.md` — concurrent doc updates
  needed. The "Phase 2 CDC handler" subsection in the package summary
  must be rewritten to describe the tick. The "60s latency" trade
  should be visible. No deep restructure.

## Affected files (docs)

- `docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md` — concurrent doc updates
  needed:
  - Top "v0 scope" callout still says "no CDC handler, no Lattice
    pulse emission, and no Reckonings book" — already wrong since the
    follow-on commissions shipped; the tick commission tightens it
    further by replacing CDC with a tick. Section needs a v1-ish
    rewrite.
  - `## Dependencies` block — add `recommends: ['clockworks']`
    (concurrent inline edit by this commission).
  - `## What the Reckoner does NOT do (in v0)` — remove "No CDC
    observer" line; replace with "No operator-configurable tick
    cadence" (brief calls this out and parks it as future work in
    `c-moixb74x`).
  - `## Schedulers` section — the "Per-evaluation config flow" /
    "Outcome mapping" / "Failure modes" sub-sections were written
    around the per-call CDC model; they need re-wording for the
    per-tick model (validateConfig throw skips the whole tick, not
    one writ; evaluate throw skips the tick; "decision carries a
    writId not in the candidate set" wording stays valid; etc.). The
    outcome-mapping table still applies.

- `docs/architecture/petitioner-registration.md` — only the sections
  that reference the CDC handler explicitly need a re-word. Most of
  the contract surface (Workflow 1 / Workflow 2, ext shape, registry
  semantics) is unchanged. Concurrent doc updates needed; spot-edit
  rather than restructure.

- `docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md` — the `tickEventId` section
  (lines 352–386) currently says "v0 always absent because the v0
  handler is CDC-only." That comment is wrong post-tick. The doc's
  prescription (stamp the triggering `clockworks.timer` event id on
  tick-driven considerations) becomes the actual behavior for the
  first time. Concurrent doc updates needed.

- `docs/architecture/clockworks.md` — the example block at line
  291 (`{ "schedule": "@every 30s", "run": "reckoner-tick" }`) is
  out-of-sync in two ways: relay name is `reckoner.tick` (dot, not
  hyphen) and the cadence is 60s in this commission. Pure example
  drift; concurrent doc updates needed if we touch the file, but
  not part of the touched set otherwise.

## Key types and interfaces (read-points, not copied verbatim)

- `Scheduler<TConfig>`, `SchedulerInput<TConfig>`, `SchedulerDecision`,
  `CapacitySnapshot`, `HeldWrit` — `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/types.ts`,
  ~340–460. Already shipped by the registry commission. The tick

  Promise<void>`. The tick reads `event.id` for `tickEventId`.

  and `getWritTypeConfig` (already used by `resolveActiveTargetPhase`).

## Adjacent patterns

- **Stdlib relay co-located with apparatus** — Clockworks's
  `summon-relay.ts` (516 lines, including the lazy-resolve / role
  check / writ-binding / template hydration / circuit breaker) and
  vision-keeper's `decline-relay.ts` (170 lines, much narrower) are
  both contributed via `apparatus.supportKit.relays`. The Reckoner's
  `reckoner.tick` follows the same pattern. The summon-relay's
  `relay({ name, description, handler })` factory call shape is the
  template.
- **Closure-scoped apparatus state shared with relay handler** — the
  Reckoner's tick handler needs `clerk`, `stacks`, `reckoningsBook`,
  `registry`, `schedulerRegistry`, `activeScheduler`, `resolveConfig`,
  `resolveSchedulerConfig`, `resolveActiveTargetPhase`,
  `alreadyConsidered`, `buildReckoningRow`. All these live in the
  `buildReckoner()` closure today. Two options for sharing them with
  a sibling-file `tick.ts`: (a) expose a small "tick context"
  parameter object the closure constructs and hands to the tick
  factory; (b) keep the tick logic in `reckoner.ts` directly. The
  summon-relay precedent uses option (a) — it lives in a sibling file
  and reaches for state via `guild()` rather than via a passed-in
  context. The Reckoner's tick reaches for state that is not on the
  guild API surface (the registry maps, the activeScheduler handle,
  the `alreadyConsidered` lookup), so option (a) — small context
  object — is the natural fit.
- **`apparatus.supportKit.standingOrders`** — no in-tree consumer
  yet; the kit-standing-orders commission shipped the substrate but
  the only sample call sites are in `clockworks.test.ts` /
  `dispatcher.test.ts` /
  `scheduler-integration.test.ts`. The Reckoner's contribution is
  the **first apparatus to ship a default standing order** (per the
  sibling commission's brief). The pattern is: declare the array on
  `apparatus.supportKit.standingOrders` exactly like
  `apparatus.supportKit.relays` and `apparatus.supportKit.events`.
- **In-tick dedupe with the existing `(writId, writUpdatedAt)`
  identity** — the Reckoner's existing per-action idempotency uses
  this pair. The tick consults `alreadyConsidered` per writ before
  invoking the scheduler so a tick that re-evaluates an
  unchanged-since-last-considered writ is a no-op. This matches the
  Sentinel's `alreadyEmitted` pattern — same shape.
- **Per-source `orderIndex` / `source` on standing-order entries** —
  the kit-standing-orders commission's `SourcedStandingOrder` shape
  carries source attribution into dispatcher / scheduler error
  messages. The Reckoner's contribution will surface as `source =
  'reckoner'` in those messages because it's the contributing plugin
  id. No Reckoner-side work; visible only in operator-facing error
  text on misconfiguration.

## Existing context

- The brief's design click `c-moiw5wkv` is concluded (sealed). It
  prescribes the relay name (`reckoner.tick`), the kit-contribution
  path, the hard-coded `@every 60s` schedule (D-5), the per-tick
  fixed sequence (D-6), and the "tick replaces CDC entirely" rule
  (D-7). No live siblings.
- `c-moixb74x` is the parked future-improvement for operator-
  configurable tick cadence — out of scope here.
- The Reckoner's `reckoner-cdc.test.ts` and `integration.test.ts` are
  the load-bearing behavioral test surfaces. The cdc test file is
  almost entirely scaffolding around CDC-driven entry points and
  re-firing-gate semantics that disappear with this commission.
- The `reckonings-book.md` doc is the contract surface for the
  `Reckonings` row shape; `tickEventId` was reserved for exactly
  this scenario.

## Doc/code discrepancies

so they are surfaced here as **concurrent doc updates needed**, not

- The example in `clockworks.md` line 291 uses `reckoner-tick` (with

  `reckoner.tick` (with dot). Out-of-touch-radius drift; concurrent
  edit if we land in clockworks.md, otherwise let the next commission
  that touches the doc fix it.

## Scope and blast radius

This commission is **internal to the Reckoner apparatus** plus a
soft (kit-contribution) edge with the Clockworks. No other apparatus
imports the Reckoner's CDC code path, so the removal blast radius is
narrowly contained inside `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/**`.

Two cross-cutting touches outside that tree:

1. **`@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus` kit surface** — the Reckoner
   becomes a new consumer of two existing kit types: `relays` (for
   the `reckoner.tick` handler) and `standingOrders` (for the
   `@every 60s` entry). Both are already-shipped substrates from the
   sibling commissions `w-moix4pe8` (kit-standing-orders) and the
   `relays` kit type that pre-dates them. The Reckoner contributes
   through its own `apparatus.supportKit` block — no Clockworks code
   changes required. Indirect dependency declaration: the Reckoner
   should add `recommends: ['clockworks']` to its apparatus descriptor
   so the dependency relation is visible to Arbor's topo sort and
   readers (concurrent doc updates needed in
   `docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md` Dependencies block).

2. **Behavioral change visible to operators / petitioners** — held
   petition latency moves from "approved on the next CDC update of the
   writ" (≈ms after `setWritExt` returns) to "approved on the next
   tick after the petition is in `new`" (≤60s). This is documented
   non-negotiable in the brief; only relevant here as a doc-touch in
   the apparatus README.

## Affected files (Reckoner package)

    `runCatchUpScan` function (lines 1146–1169); the call to

    test-hook surface entries `handleWritsChange` and `runCatchUpScan`
    on `ReckonerTestHooks` (interface lines 250–256, hook-object lines
    1375–1376).

    takes a single `(writ, ext, now)` triple and builds

    `considerWrit` (lines 795–876) — the brief says "becomes the

  `createReckonerTickRelay()` factory and the pure tick-evaluation

  (`'malformed' | 'duplicate' | 'policy_violation' | 'source_banned' |
  'source_unregistered' | 'other'`) — same file, ~225. The disabled-
  source path may newly emit `source_banned` (currently unused) per
  D2.
- `RelayDefinition`, `RelayHandler`, `RelayContext`, `GuildEvent` —
  `packages/plugins/clockworks/src/relay.ts`. The tick is a
  `RelayDefinition`; the handler signature is
  `(event: GuildEvent | null, context: RelayContext) => void |
  Promise<void>`. The tick reads `event.id` for `tickEventId`.
- `StandingOrder` —
  `packages/plugins/clockworks/src/types.ts`, ~116. The kit
  contribution is one of these: `{ schedule: '@every 60s', run:
  'reckoner.tick' }`.
- `ClockworksKit.standingOrders?: StandingOrder[]` and
  `ClockworksKit.relays?: RelayDefinition[]` — same file, ~505–541.
  The Reckoner's apparatus.supportKit fills both slots (alongside the
  existing `books`, `tools` if any, `events` if any, and `schedulers`
  slot already populated).
- `WritDoc`, `WritPhase`, `WritTypeConfig` — Clerk surface; the tick
  reuses `clerk.transition`, `clerk.show` (indirect via the reads),
  and `getWritTypeConfig` (already used by `resolveActiveTargetPhase`).

## Adjacent patterns

- **Stdlib relay co-located with apparatus** — Clockworks's
  `summon-relay.ts` (516 lines, including the lazy-resolve / role
  check / writ-binding / template hydration / circuit breaker) and
  vision-keeper's `decline-relay.ts` (170 lines, much narrower) are
  both contributed via `apparatus.supportKit.relays`. The Reckoner's
  `reckoner.tick` follows the same pattern. The summon-relay's
  `relay({ name, description, handler })` factory call shape is the
  template.
- **Closure-scoped apparatus state shared with relay handler** — the
  Reckoner's tick handler needs `clerk`, `stacks`, `reckoningsBook`,
  `registry`, `schedulerRegistry`, `activeScheduler`, `resolveConfig`,
  `resolveSchedulerConfig`, `resolveActiveTargetPhase`,
  `alreadyConsidered`, `buildReckoningRow`. All these live in the
  `buildReckoner()` closure today. Two options for sharing them with
  a sibling-file `tick.ts`: (a) expose a small "tick context"
  parameter object the closure constructs and hands to the tick
  factory; (b) keep the tick logic in `reckoner.ts` directly. The
  summon-relay precedent uses option (a) — it lives in a sibling file
  and reaches for state via `guild()` rather than via a passed-in
  context. The Reckoner's tick reaches for state that is not on the
  guild API surface (the registry maps, the activeScheduler handle,
  the `alreadyConsidered` lookup), so option (a) — small context
  object — is the natural fit.
- **`apparatus.supportKit.standingOrders`** — no in-tree consumer
  yet; the kit-standing-orders commission shipped the substrate but
  the only sample call sites are in `clockworks.test.ts` /
  `dispatcher.test.ts` /
  `scheduler-integration.test.ts`. The Reckoner's contribution is
  the **first apparatus to ship a default standing order** (per the
  sibling commission's brief). The pattern is: declare the array on
  `apparatus.supportKit.standingOrders` exactly like
  `apparatus.supportKit.relays` and `apparatus.supportKit.events`.
- **In-tick dedupe with the existing `(writId, writUpdatedAt)`
  identity** — the Reckoner's existing per-action idempotency uses
  this pair. The tick consults `alreadyConsidered` per writ before
  invoking the scheduler so a tick that re-evaluates an
  unchanged-since-last-considered writ is a no-op. This matches the
  Sentinel's `alreadyEmitted` pattern — same shape.
- **Per-source `orderIndex` / `source` on standing-order entries** —
  the kit-standing-orders commission's `SourcedStandingOrder` shape
  carries source attribution into dispatcher / scheduler error
  messages. The Reckoner's contribution will surface as `source =
  'reckoner'` in those messages because it's the contributing plugin
  id. No Reckoner-side work; visible only in operator-facing error
  text on misconfiguration.

## Existing context

- The brief's design click `c-moiw5wkv` is concluded (sealed). It
  prescribes the relay name (`reckoner.tick`), the kit-contribution
  path, the hard-coded `@every 60s` schedule (D-5), the per-tick
  fixed sequence (D-6), and the "tick replaces CDC entirely" rule
  (D-7). No live siblings.
- `c-moixb74x` is the parked future-improvement for operator-
  configurable tick cadence — out of scope here.
- The Reckoner's `reckoner-cdc.test.ts` and `integration.test.ts` are
  the load-bearing behavioral test surfaces. The cdc test file is
  almost entirely scaffolding around CDC-driven entry points and
  re-firing-gate semantics that disappear with this commission.
- The `reckonings-book.md` doc is the contract surface for the
  `Reckonings` row shape; `tickEventId` was reserved for exactly
  this scenario.

## Doc/code discrepancies

(All the following are in files this commission will already touch,
so they are surfaced here as **concurrent doc updates needed**, not
lifted as observations.)

- `docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md` top callout still says
  "v0 ships the contract surface only — no CDC handler, no Lattice
  pulse emission, and no Reckonings book". That has been wrong for
  multiple commissions. The tick commission either leaves it (it's
  out of touch radius) or catches up the callout block when editing
  Dependencies / Schedulers sections.
- The `tickEventId?: string` field in `types.ts` (line 308) carries a
  comment ending with "The v0 handler is CDC-only, so this field is
  always absent on v0 rows." The tick commission flips that — the
  field is now stamped on every tick-driven row.
- The Reckoner README (lines 41–58) describes the Phase 2 CDC
  handler in detail. After this commission the handler is gone; the
  description should describe the tick.
- The example in `clockworks.md` line 291 uses `reckoner-tick` (with
  hyphen) as the relay name. The brief's relay name is
  `reckoner.tick` (with dot). Out-of-touch-radius drift; concurrent
  edit if we land in clockworks.md, otherwise let the next commission
  that touches the doc fix it.

lastPrompt: can you tell if the files being read into context needed to be, because they were edited or somehow impacted the edits? I'm concerned that the files list from the astrolabe may be forcing unnecessary…

content.text:
Sean, your hypothesis is empirically correct — and the asymmetry between the two rigs is striking.

## Read-utilization findings

| Rig | Total Read content | Read-and-edited | Read but NEVER touched | Pure-read share |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Rig 1** (cleanup) | 144K chars | 141K chars | 2.7K chars (1 file) | **1.9%** |
| **Rig 2** (reckoner tick) | 459K chars | 234K chars | **225K chars (13 files)** | **49.1%** |

Rig 2's implementer pulled **~56K tokens** of pure context bloat into its session — files read for *understanding* but never edited. That bloat then compounded across 147 turns × cache replay ≈ **~8M of the 40.8M cumulative cache reads were paying to re-read files the agent never modified**. That's roughly 20% of the rig's total cache cost spent on context the agent didn't act on.

## The 13 pure-read files in rig 2

| File | Chars | Category | Why it was probably read |
|---|---:|---|---|
| `docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md` | 62K | doc | Spec referenced `tickEventId` field in this doc |
| `clockworks/src/clockworks.ts` | 44K | source | Cross-package "how clockworks works" |
| `reckoner/src/reckoner.test.ts` | 34K | test | Spec said "(not opened) ... should pass unchanged" — implementer opened it anyway |
| `clockworks/src/types.ts` | 27K | source | Listed as "read-point" for `StandingOrder` type |
| `clockworks/src/summon-relay.ts` | 23K | source | Listed as "Adjacent pattern" — pattern to mirror |
| `reckoner/README.md` | 10K | doc | "Concurrent doc updates needed" but didn't end up edited |
| `clockworks/src/relay.ts` | 8.6K | source | Listed as "read-point" for `RelayDefinition` type |
| `vision-keeper/src/decline-relay.ts` | 8.2K | source | Listed as "Adjacent pattern" (and **vestigial** — this plugin was deleted in rig 1) |
| `clockworks/src/index.ts` | 3K | source | Probably for `export` lookup |
| `reckoner/src/schedulers/always-approve.ts` | 2.2K | source | "No changes" but agent peeked anyway |
| `reckoner/src/index.ts` | 2.1K | source | Same — "no changes expected" |
| `vision-keeper/src/vision-keeper.ts` | 1.9K | source | More vestigial vision-keeper |
| `reckoner/package.json` | 0.3K | config | "No changes expected" but agent looked |

## I went and read the astrolabe inventory for rig 2 — it's the smoking gun

The plan inventory for `w-moiy8hkv` (rig 2) contains explicit sections that *direct* the implementer to read files for understanding without editing:

**Section: "Key types and interfaces (read-points, not copied verbatim)"** lists exactly:

- `clockworks/src/relay.ts` for `RelayDefinition`, `RelayHandler`, `RelayContext`, `GuildEvent`
- `clockworks/src/types.ts` for `StandingOrder`, `ClockworksKit`
- `reckoner/src/types.ts` for `Scheduler`, `SchedulerInput`, etc.

**Section: "Adjacent patterns"** lists exactly:

- `clockworks/src/summon-relay.ts` ("the template")
- `vision-keeper/src/decline-relay.ts` ("much narrower")

**Section: "Affected files (Reckoner package)"** lists files marked "no changes expected" but doesn't tell the implementer *not* to read them:

- `reckoner/src/index.ts` — "no changes expected"
- `reckoner/src/reckoner.test.ts` — "(not opened) ... should pass unchanged"
- `reckoner/src/schedulers/always-approve.ts` — "no changes"
- `reckoner/package.json` — "no changes expected"

**The implementer read every single one.**

The inventory is doing the right thing *conceptually* — telling the implementer where the relevant type definitions and pattern templates live — but it's communicating "go look at this file" when what it actually needs is "here's the type signature inline" or "here's the pattern in 30 lines."

## Why rig 1 didn't suffer

Rig 1's task was mechanical: delete a plugin, rename strings, rewrite §11 of a doc. The inventory for that kind of task names files-to-modify, not files-to-understand. Almost every Read mapped 1:1 to a subsequent Edit.

Substantive code changes (rig 2 type) are where the inventory pattern bites. The agent legitimately needs to know *how* clockworks works to register a new tick relay correctly. The inventory tells it "read `clockworks.ts` for the relay registration pattern" — and the agent reads all 44K chars of `clockworks.ts`.

## Concrete mitigations, ordered by leverage

### 1. Inline type signatures and pattern excerpts in the inventory

Instead of:

> *"Read `clockworks/src/types.ts` for `StandingOrder` shape (line ~116)"*

Have the reader-analyst emit:

> *"`StandingOrder` shape (from `clockworks/src/types.ts:116`):*
> ```ts
> interface StandingOrder {
>   schedule: string
>   run: string
>   id?: string
>   ...
> }
> ```
> *"Reckoner's contribution: `{ schedule: '@every 60s', run: 'reckoner.tick' }`."*

That's 200 chars of inventory replacing 27K chars of full-file Read. **A 100x reduction on each "read-point" entry.** And it works fine for the implementer — types and patterns are tiny in the spec, big in the file.

### 2. Inline pattern templates with surrounding context

Instead of *"see `summon-relay.ts` as the template,"* the inventory could include:

> *"Pattern (from `summon-relay.ts`, abbreviated):*
> ```ts
> export function createSummonRelay(deps: { ... }): RelayDefinition {
>   return relay({ name: 'summon', description: '...', handler: async (event, ctx) => { ... } })
> }
> ```
> *"Apply to `tick.ts`: identical shape, dependencies are `{ clerk, stacks, reckoningsBook, registry, scheduler, ... }`."*

The implementer doesn't need 23K chars of `summon-relay.ts` source to follow that pattern. 30 lines of pattern excerpt suffices.

### 3. Strict "do-not-read" markers on listed-but-unchanged files

For files the inventory mentions but the agent shouldn't open:

> *"`reckoner/src/index.ts` — no changes. **Do not Read.**"*
> *"`reckoner/package.json` — no changes. **Do not Read.**"*

Belt-and-suspenders: the spec already says "no changes expected" — but the implementer still pulled them in. An explicit "Do not Read" might be enough.

### 4. Vestigial reference cleanup

`vision-keeper/src/decline-relay.ts` was cited as a pattern even though that plugin was about to be (and in rig 1 was) deleted. The inventory was generated against a worktree where vision-keeper still existed. **Reader-analyst should detect "this file is up for deletion" and not cite it as a pattern.**

### 5. Adopt subagents for "study this file" reads

When the implementer truly needs to absorb a large file, dispatch a subagent (Task tool) that reads it and returns a 1K summary. The 44K chars of `clockworks.ts` stay in the subagent's context, not the parent's.

## Estimated savings if inventory was excerpted instead of pointer-based

For rig 2, if the 56K-token pure-read content were replaced with 5K of inline excerpts:

- Final context: 375K → ~324K (drop ~51K)
- Average context per turn: 266K → ~230K
- Cumulative cache reads: 40.8M → ~33M
- Cost: $25.86 → ~$21 (saves ~$5, ~20%)

That's a substantial single-knob improvement *without* any structural changes — no checkpointing, no model swap, no repo split. Just reformatting the inventory output.

## Implication for the X010 / H4 work

This is a **separate cost lever from H4**. The H4 work targets cumulative-context replay across long sessions; the inventory-bloat finding targets *what gets loaded into context in the first place*. Both apply, and they multiply:

- **Inventory excerpting alone** could cut implementer cost ~20% on substantive commissions
- **H4 handoff splitting alone** could cut cost ~33% on long sessions
- **Both together** could plausibly compound to ~50% reduction

Want me to:

1. **Add a section to the X010 simulation artifact** quantifying the inventory-bloat finding and tying it to the cost model?
2. **Open a separate click** specifically for "inventory format reform" so the reader-analyst's output gets restructured?
3. **Sample more rigs** (especially substantive code-change ones) to see whether rig 2's 49% pure-read share is typical or extreme for that task class?

The first two are cheap. The third would broaden the evidence base.

content: We're investigating cost drivers in the implement engine for X010's H4 work. Just identified that ~49% of rig 2's read content was never edited — pure inventory-driven context bloat. Awaiting your call on three follow-ups: extend the artifact, open an inventory-reform click, or broaden the sample.

message.content: can you list out all the practical ideas to reduce turns, unnecessary reads, and overall cost which we've accumulated in this conversation?

content.text:
Here's the consolidated list of practical interventions. Organized by category, ordered roughly by impact-to-effort ratio within each group. I've added a rough estimate column for what each is worth based on the rigs we analyzed — these are directional, not measured.

## 1. Model selection — biggest single dial

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 1 | **Switch implementer from Opus to Sonnet** | Output tokens are most expensive; cache reads are secondary. Sonnet is ~5x cheaper across all token lanes. | ~70-80% on implement | trivial (config) |
| 2 | **Route by task complexity** | Small/mechanical commissions → Sonnet; architectural ones → Opus. Run a quality-equivalence test first. | varies, large in aggregate | small (heuristic) |

This category alone could halve current commission costs with minimal risk.

## 2. Spec & inventory format — newly identified this session

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 3 | **Inline type signatures in the inventory** | Replace "see `types.ts` for `StandingOrder`" with the actual type def (200 chars). Avoids 27K-char full-file Reads. | ~15-20% on substantive code commissions | small (sage-writer prompt) |
| 4 | **Inline pattern templates** | Replace "see `summon-relay.ts` as template" with a 30-line excerpt of the pattern. | ~5-10% on cross-pattern work | small |
| 5 | **"Do not Read" markers on no-change files** | Files listed in inventory as "no changes expected" still get Read by curious implementers. Mark them explicitly. | ~5% | trivial |
| 6 | **Strip vestigial pattern references** | Reader-analyst should detect "this file is up for deletion" and not cite it as a reference pattern. | small but specific | small |
| 7 | **Pre-quote source excerpts in spec** | If the spec says "rewrite §11 of `petitioner-registration.md`", inline §11 in the spec rather than making implementer Read the whole 41K-char doc. | ~10% on doc-edit work | medium (sage-writer redesign) |

Items 3–7 collectively could cut substantive-commission cost by ~20-30% with no architectural changes. Pure prompt/format work in the sage-writer + reader-analyst engines.

## 3. Implementer behavior — prompt-level nudges

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 8 | **Prefer Bash bulk edits for systematic renames** | Replace 10–20 sequential Edit calls with one `sed -i` / `find -exec`. Saves turns and tool-result accumulation. | ~5-10% on rename-heavy work | small (prompt nudge) |
| 9 | **Targeted Reads (offset + limit)** | After a Grep returns line numbers, Read just the surrounding range (200 lines), not the full file. | ~5-15% | small (prompt) |
| 10 | **Discourage repeat greps** | Same patterns re-searched 4+ times per session. Implementer should track its own search history. | ~2-5% | small but hard to enforce |
| 11 | **Narrow test filters first, full suite only as final gate** | Implementer ran `pnpm -w test` 6+ times in rig 2. Should run `pnpm --filter <pkg> test` for iterative feedback, full suite once at the end. | ~10-15% wall time on substantive work | small (prompt) |
| 12 | **Don't re-test packages you didn't change** | Avoid running tests on adjacent packages "just to be safe." | ~5% wall time | small |

## 4. Test infrastructure

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 13 | **Test impact analysis (turborepo / nx)** | Only run tests for changed packages and their dependents. Cuts the 2-min `pnpm -w test` to seconds for most commissions. Saves both wall time AND context bloat (smaller test output). | ~10-15% cost, ~50% wall time | medium (CI rework) |
| 14 | **Test design audit** | The 7213-second hung test in rig 2 was a defect, not a scale issue. Sweep for tests with timer/sleep dependencies. | one-time fix; eliminates outlier costs | medium |

## 5. Engine pipeline decomposition

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 15 | **Checkpoint-and-fresh-session implement** (`c-modxxtu6`) | End implement after each manifest task, start fresh with compact handoff. Targets the ~50% savings the simulation predicted. | ~30-50% on long sessions | large (architecture) |
| 16 | **Keep handoff under ~30K tokens** | Empirically the savings vector is `(B - H)`. Bigger handoffs = smaller savings. Target git refs + manifest progress + active-task pointer only. | binding constraint on #15 | n/a |
| 17 | **Suppress per-session orientation tax** | Each fresh session must produce productive work in <5 turns. Otherwise turn-count expansion eats the savings (3.5× expansion was the X010 H1 failure mode). | binding constraint on #15 | medium (handoff design) |
| 18 | **Subdivide implement into core + finalize** | Even simpler: split implement into "write + green per-package tests" and "fix full-suite + update docs." Two sessions, one handoff. | ~30-40% on substantive commissions | medium |
| 19 | **Coarser checkpointing for small manifests** | Per-task checkpoint hurts when tasks are <30 turns each. Checkpoint every 2–3 tasks instead. | tunes #15 to small commissions | small |

## 6. Subagent decomposition

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 20 | **Subagent for big-file analysis** | When implementer needs to "understand" a 50K-char file, dispatch a Task subagent that returns a 1K summary. Big content stays out of parent context. | ~10-15% on substantive work | small (prompt addition) |
| 21 | **Subagent for cross-package source** | "How does clockworks integrate with ext events?" becomes a 1K integration brief, not 84K of cross-package source Reads. | ~10-15% on cross-package work | small |

Items 20–21 are subsumed by items 3–4 (inline excerpting in spec) when those work, but subagents are a useful escape hatch when the spec didn't anticipate the question.

## 7. Architectural patterns

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 22 | **Boundary-survey engine** | New astrolabe stage that runs after spec-writer, producing a focused integration brief for cross-package work. Costs one small session, saves 20K+ tokens of permanent context downstream. | ~10-15% on cross-package work | medium (new engine) |
| 23 | **Published types as integration contract** | Long-term, plugins should consume framework via `.d.ts`, not source. Reduces grep surface and forces clean interfaces. | small immediate, large structural | large (discipline + tooling) |

## 8. Repo structure (last resort)

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 24 | **Polyrepo split** | Forces clean boundaries; enables per-repo CI scoping; smaller worktrees. **But:** addresses symptoms more cheaply solvable with #13 + #22. | overlaps with #13 + #22 | very large |
| 25 | **Read-only sibling mounts** | If we go polyrepo: mount framework + plugin repos into devcontainer for grep access. Smaller writable worktree, broader read surface. | n/a — coupled with #24 | medium |
| 26 | **`.agent-context/` per repo** | If we go polyrepo: each repo ships a curated context bundle (types, patterns, recent changes). | n/a — coupled with #24 | medium ongoing |

## 9. Upstream / Claude Code limitations

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 27 | **Context eviction primitive** | Currently Claude Code retains all tool results forever. A "drop this Read result, I'm done with it" affordance would cut the carry-forward tax directly. | very large if available | not in our control |
| 28 | **Auto-compaction tuning** | The 2026-04-16 baseline showed auto-compaction works gracefully. We could deliberately invoke it earlier as a poor man's checkpoint. | n/a — already happening | small (test) |

## 10. Operational

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 29 | **Rate-limit / rebase friction reduction** | Rig 2 lost ~4 hours to rate-limit pause + manual-merge spider. Independent of everything above. Worth a separate inquiry. | varies, can be huge | medium |

---

## Compounding view

The interventions stack. Rough multiplicative model on a rig-2-shape commission ($25.86 implementer cost):

| Stack | Savings | Resulting cost |
|---|---:|---:|
| Baseline | 0% | $25.86 |
| + Sonnet (#1) | ~75% | $6.50 |
| + Inventory excerpting (#3, #4, #7) | additional ~20% | $5.20 |
| + Test scoping (#13) | additional ~10% | $4.70 |
| + Handoff split (#15) on 150-turn equivalent | additional ~30% | $3.30 |

Roughly **8x cost reduction** if everything stacks at expected rates. Even half that compounding (4x) would dramatically change the per-commission economics.

## Recommended sequencing

If I were prioritizing what to actually do, in order:

1. **Sonnet test (#1)** — one knob, biggest impact, reversible. Run a current-shape commission both ways, compare quality.
2. **Inventory excerpting (#3, #4, #5, #7)** — pure prompt work in sage-writer/reader-analyst. No architecture changes. Quick wins.
3. **Test scoping (#13)** — install turborepo, configure pipelines. Saves wall time everywhere. Compounds with everything else.
4. **Handoff split prototype (#15-#19)** — once #1-#3 are in place and you've measured the residual cost, use the simulation's predicted ~33% savings as the target. Falsifiable test, then iterate.
5. **Polyrepo (#24)** — only if #13 + #22 + boundaries fail to deliver. Last resort because it's expensive to do and undo.

Items 8–12 (implementer prompt nudges) are basically free and worth bundling with whichever sage-side change ships first.

The polyrepo conversation is where this thread started, and it's the right answer to ask but probably the wrong intervention to reach for first. The data says: most of the cost is *avoidable* through cheaper changes, and only what's left after those should drive structural decisions.

lastPrompt: can you list out all the practical ideas to reduce turns, unnecessary reads, and overall cost which we've accumulated in this conversation?

message.content: capture that in a .scratch doc for cost optimization. then find or create a relevant top-level click, and recreate your breakdown as child clicks/grandchild clicks. then create additional clicks under there for 'priority 1 - inventory excerpting', and link to #3, #4, #5, #7. Create 'priority 2 - test scoping', 'priority 3 - handoff split protype' as well. Then give me a summary of what experiment updates we should make so i can approve those. finally, consider the 'birepo' case, where we have a 'core' repo of framework packages that are essentially considered sealed, and another 'plugins' repo of pieces that are still in active development

# c-modxx4nj-9cf3bcf5762c [live]
> Which intervention(s) should we pursue to reduce implement cost without losing the quality gains?
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-25T06:10:19.471Z

## c-modxxqwg-c441293b52f0 [live]
> Split implement and verify into separate rig stages — implement writes+commits (no tests), verify runs the suite in a fresh context-free session and posts patch mandates on failure
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-25T06:10:48.304Z

## c-modxxsch-b1f947a7bd5f [concluded]
> Narrow verify commands in the task manifest to package-scoped (pnpm --filter <pkg> test) instead of full monorepo runs, to cut tool_result size and execution time
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Selected as the first intervention under one-change-at-a-time discipline; dispatched as commission w-modyiet1-2b3fa5419183 (codex nexus, complexity 1). See sibling c-modyhqk3 for the dispatch-decision record (full reasoning over the six options).
Superseded by: c-modz11rc "Package-scoped verify commands — REFUTED as a cost lever. Per-verify output volume measured at 1.9KB avg pre and post Apr-16 (identical), accounting for ~0.9% of transcript size. Agents were already running --filter and tail-piped commands organically; codifying the discipline in sage-writer.md would not change behaviour. Original commission w-modyiet1 cancelled before pickup. The cost driver is turn count (103→166), turn count is task-count driven, not verify-driven."
Resolved by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T06:10:50.177Z
Resolved: 2026-04-25T06:27:56.659Z

## c-modxxtu6-1724573883b0 [live]
> Checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture for implement — end the session after each task commit and start a fresh one with a compact handoff to bound cache-read accumulation
Status: live
Created: 2026-04-25T06:10:52.110Z

## c-modxxvef-08445ef7d3c5 [live]
> Defer all verification to seal — implement just writes+commits, seal runs tests first and posts a narrow patch mandate on failure (cheaper but loses mid-task diagnostic context)
Status: live

## c-moe0l7bl-d93c2fd660f1 [live]
> Predicted-files gate at the planning stage — count distinct paths in the manifest <files> elements; if the count exceeds a threshold (~15), halt the planning pipeline and emit a gap report asking the patron to decompose the brief. Empirically the strongest single cost lever found in the Apr-25 deep-dive: 11% of post-Apr-16 implement sessions touch >=20 actual files, but they account for ~38% of total implement cost. Manifest predictions are within 1.5x of actual ~75% of the time, so a 15-pred bound is roughly equivalent to a 20-actual bound. Mechanism is direct (every distinct file the implementer reads lands in cached context); enforcement is trivial (regex over manifest XML). Distinct from c-modxxyfz task-count cap — this gates on the actual mechanical predictor, not a proxy. The two interventions overlap heavily but predicted-files-gate is more precise.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:25:01.905Z

### c-moe0lgs1-b0412bbefd73 [live]
> Cost cliff finding (Apr-25, n=74 post-Apr-16 implement sessions joined to seal commits): cost grows roughly linearly with files touched up through ~9 files (~$1/file slope), plateaus at $7-9 from 10-19 files, then jumps 3.2x at 20+ files (mean $27.21, median $27.63). On the predicted side the cliff is at 15 predicted files (mean $29.63, median $35.64). 8/74 sessions sit above the actual-files cliff and account for ~38% of total post-Apr-16 implement cost (~$217 of $568). Files-touched is also the single strongest cost predictor we measured (Pearson +0.81 vs cost) — beats spec_chars (+0.74), task count (+0.53), and churn-LOC (+0.73). The cliff shape supports a hard threshold rather than a continuous incentive: most expensive sessions are above the cliff, most cheap sessions are well below; very few sit in the danger zone, suggesting agents naturally either stay narrow or blow well past.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:25:14.161Z

#### c-moe1tgmo-6226f1a522ad [live]
> Multi-metric cliff catalog (Apr-25, n=70): in addition to the n_files cliff at 17 (22% of cost), three other metrics show clean cliffs that could form alternative or supplementary gates. total_cross_pkg >= 50 (10% of sessions, 36% of cost; sharpest single cliff, $11 → $26 jump). total_imports >= 80 (13% of sessions, 50% of cost; biggest cost share captured). total_exports >= 65 and total_types >= 80 each catch ~25% of cost in 6% of sessions; smaller fraction caught but dramatic per-session (0+ medians). Smooth-ramp metrics that don't make good cliff-gates but matter linearly: total_branches (cyclomatic proxy), total_loc, max_loc. Implications: a single n_files gate catches less cost than a more mechanism-direct gate would; multiple gates could compose; cross-package imports specifically points at coupling as the cost mechanism, not just code volume.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:59:26.832Z

### c-moe0lmhy-f317635ad959 [live]
> Manifest blast-radius prediction accuracy (Apr-25, n=60 sessions with both manifest <files> data and seal commits): median actual/predicted file ratio = 1.00x (perfect), 76% of sessions within 1.5x either way, 12% optimistic (under-predict by >1.5x), 12% pessimistic. Package-level precision 95% / recall 88% (sage correctly names which packages will be touched, occasionally misses one). The optimistic sessions cluster in the high-cost tail — for sessions that ended up touching 20+ files, the planner predicted 9-17, ratio 1.3-2.7x. Implication: a predicted-files gate at 15 would catch most-but-not-all of the 20+ actual sessions (some would slip through with predictions of 10-14 that actually became 20+). Could pair the gate with a runtime check during implement (if actual files-touched grows past N, halt and ask). Or trust 76% accuracy and accept some leakage.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:25:21.574Z

### c-moe1b73b-fdce8030f251 [live]
> Non-LLM blast-radius pre-check — gate on predicted files BEFORE running the expensive sage-writer stage. Two strategies: (1) cheap heuristic over the brief — symbol-name search across .d.ts files / package.json exports, package-name extraction from Scope sections, explicit-path regex — likely low recall on sparse briefs but free to run. (2) restructure the planning pipeline so the inventory/file-list step happens first, gate fires after that cheap stage, before the expensive writer expands the spec. Pipeline today: reader-analyst (lists inventory + makes decisions) -> spec-writer (expands to full spec). Inserting a gate between these two stages catches over-large commissions before paying the writer's cost. The reader-analyst output already includes inventory; we'd just need to count predicted files there and halt-or-decompose if over threshold. Cleaner architecturally than gating after spec-publish since it saves the writer-stage cost on rejected commissions.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:45:14.663Z

### c-moe1bd71-015ca0003020 [live]
> Self-decomposing planner — instead of failing/warning when predicted files exceed the gate threshold, the planner automatically partitions the manifest's tasks into groups whose collective file-touches fit under the limit, then dispatches each group as a child commission. Required substrate: (a) per-task <files> data (already in the manifest); (b) bin-packing-style grouping that respects task dependency ordering (some tasks must precede others); (c) Spider follows-link wiring to express dependency between child commissions; (d) parent-commission completion semantics — when does the parent close? When all children complete, or eagerly? Open questions: how does the patron see what got auto-decomposed? Does the original brief get a pointer to the spawned children? What if the partition produces unbalanced loads (one child with 3 tasks, another with 1)? Fits as a sibling or child of the predicted-files-gate (c-moe0l7bl) — same gating signal, different response. Bigger lift, larger payoff (no patron intervention required for big briefs).
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:45:22.573Z

### c-moe1tb5k-fb33783b108d [live]
> Three-tier gate metric evolution — start with file count, evolve toward more mechanism-direct measures. v0: predicted_files >= 15 (regex paths from manifest, captures ~22% of post-Apr-16 cost). v1: predicted_imports >= 80 (for each predicted file, grep import lines, sum across files; captures ~50% of cost). v2: predicted_cross_pkg_imports >= 50 (for each predicted file, grep '@shardworks/' imports, sum; captures ~36% of cost — sharpest cliff at the threshold). All three are static analyses using only the predicted file list + the draft worktree contents — no LLM calls needed beyond the planner. v1 captures the biggest cost fraction; v2 is sharpest at the cliff and most mechanism-direct (each cross-package edge IS the coupling cost). v0 is operationally simplest (no file IO at planning time). Suggested progression: ship v0 first, instrument v1 and v2 as observers (compute and log but don't gate), validate the cliff thresholds in production, then convert the strongest observer to enforcing.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:59:19.736Z

## c-moe1zydb-1782850ec6b7 [live]
> Commit-time complexity ratchet — quality check at the review/seal stage that rejects agent commits which push touched files past cost-driving complexity thresholds. Distinct from the planning gate (c-moe0l7bl) which prevents expensive commissions from running; this prevents expensive-making changes from landing. Together they bound both ends — what work dispatches, what state the codebase ends up in. Design: ratchet rather than absolute thresholds (only allow changes in the improving direction; legacy files frozen at current state, no further debt accumulated, no blocked work to shrink them). Threshold candidates from Apr-25 per-file data (n=171 touched files): file_LOC > 2500, imports > 12, cross_pkg_lines > 8, cross_pkg_symbols > 13 (set at p95 of observed distribution). Implementation home: review engine (after implement commits, before seal). Computes per-file metrics on diffed files, fails review with structured feedback if any threshold is tripped without ratchet improvement. Open questions: should grandfathered legacy files be visible (a per-file 'high-water mark' shown alongside the diff)? Should the check produce a structured 'budget exceeded' artifact vs unstructured review prose? Should it be soft (warning in review) or hard (review fail)? Likely soft-first to validate thresholds, then hard.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T08:04:29.759Z

Required substrate: (a) per-task <files> data (already in the manifest); (b) bin-packing-style grouping that respects task dependency ordering (some tasks must precede others); (c) Spider follows-link wiring to express dependency between child commissions; (d) parent-commission completion semantics — when does the parent close? When all children complete, or eagerly? Open questions: how does the patron see what got auto-decomposed? Does the original brief get a pointer to the spawned children? What if the partition produces unbalanced loads (one child with 3 tasks, another with 1)? Fits as a sibling or child of the predicted-files-gate (c-moe0l7bl) — same gating signal, different response. Bigger lift, larger payoff (no patron intervention required for big briefs).
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:45:22.573Z

### c-moe1tb5k-fb33783b108d [live]
> Three-tier gate metric evolution — start with file count, evolve toward more mechanism-direct measures. v0: predicted_files >= 15 (regex paths from manifest, captures ~22% of post-Apr-16 cost). v1: predicted_imports >= 80 (for each predicted file, grep import lines, sum across files; captures ~50% of cost). v2: predicted_cross_pkg_imports >= 50 (for each predicted file, grep '@shardworks/' imports, sum; captures ~36% of cost — sharpest cliff at the threshold). All three are static analyses using only the predicted file list + the draft worktree contents — no LLM calls needed beyond the planner. v1 captures the biggest cost fraction; v2 is sharpest at the cliff and most mechanism-direct (each cross-package edge IS the coupling cost). v0 is operationally simplest (no file IO at planning time). Suggested progression: ship v0 first, instrument v1 and v2 as observers (compute and log but don't gate), validate the cliff thresholds in production, then convert the strongest observer to enforcing.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T07:59:19.736Z

### c-moecggl6-0b646d6d6619 [concluded]
> Predicted-files gate v0 (soft-warn) commission dispatched as w-moecg331-fd7efad8df99 (codex nexus, complexity 2). Adds manifestFilesCount to PlanDoc, emits astrolabe.plan.files-over-threshold Clockworks event when count exceeds configurable threshold (default 15). No halt, no coupling to laboratory — framework records the signal, sanctum-side instrumentation can subscribe to the event for alerting/dashboarding/etc. Sets up the v1/v2 evolution path captured in c-moe1tb5k (richer signals once we have v0 data validating the threshold).
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Predicted-files gate v0 (soft-warn) landed via manual recovery — work succeeded through implement+review+revise, but seal failed 3x due to concurrent-seal contention with four other commissions running simultaneously. Patron rebased the draft branch onto current main (no conflicts) and pushed directly as 047a0927 (astrolabe: add predicted-files-touched soft-warn signal at plan-finalize). Implementation matches brief: PlanDoc.manifestFilesCount field, astrolabe.predictedFilesThreshold config (default 15), manifest-files.ts parser, astrolabe.plan.files-over-threshold Clockworks event emitted strictly after the patch lands with [astrolabe] console.warn breadcrumbs. Writ w-moecg331 stays in 'failed' phase since failed is terminal. Concurrent-seal contention pattern is data for the cost-drift sentinel work (c-modzrgiu) — three retries giving up signals a real coordination-layer fragility.
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Resolved by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T12:57:16.026Z
Resolved: 2026-04-25T21:03:50.952Z

## c-moe1zydb-1782850ec6b7 [live]
> Commit-time complexity ratchet — quality check at the review/seal stage that rejects agent commits which push touched files past cost-driving complexity thresholds. Distinct from the planning gate (c-moe0l7bl) which prevents expensive commissions from running; this prevents expensive-making changes from landing. Together they bound both ends — what work dispatches, what state the codebase ends up in. Design: ratchet rather than absolute thresholds (only allow changes in the improving direction; legacy files frozen at current state, no further debt accumulated, no blocked work to shrink them). Threshold candidates from Apr-25 per-file data (n=171 touched files): file_LOC > 2500, imports > 12, cross_pkg_lines > 8, cross_pkg_symbols > 13 (set at p95 of observed distribution). Implementation home: review engine (after implement commits, before seal). Computes per-file metrics on diffed files, fails review with structured feedback if any threshold is tripped without ratchet improvement. Open questions: should grandfathered legacy files be visible (a per-file 'high-water mark' shown alongside the diff)? Should the check produce a structured 'budget exceeded' artifact vs unstructured review prose? Should it be soft (warning in review) or hard (review fail)? Likely soft-first to validate thresholds, then hard.
Status: live
Created by: 003e859f-be32-46f9-9dd0-0d0a3b91a968
Created: 2026-04-25T08:04:29.759Z

toolUseResult.content:
# Cost-Optimization Landscape — Apr 29 2026

A consolidated reference catalog of all the practical interventions for
reducing implementer cost (turns, unnecessary reads, cache-read drag,
total $) accumulated during the Apr 29 polyrepo→cost-economics
conversation. Source data came from analyzing two recent rigs
(`rig-moji64hs` vision-keeper cleanup, `rig-moj12h4o` reckoner tick) and
mining 101 archived implement transcripts.

This document is the canonical reference. The click tree under
`c-modxx4nj` carries the same structure for trackability.

## Estimates context

All "savings" estimates below are directional projections from the
analyzed rigs, not measured outcomes. Where comparable, costs reference
rig 2 (Reckoner tick) at $25.86 implementer cost as a typical
substantive-commission baseline.

---

## 1. Model selection

The single largest cost dial. Output tokens cost ~50× cache reads, and
implement is most expensive when mechanical work runs on Opus.

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 1 | **Switch implementer from Opus to Sonnet** | Output and cache-read prices both drop ~5×. Most implement work is mechanical edits. | ~70-80% | trivial (config) |
| 2 | **Route by task complexity** | Small/mechanical → Sonnet; architectural → Opus. Run a quality-equivalence test first. | varies | small (heuristic) |

## 2. Spec & inventory format — newly identified Apr 29

The reader-analyst's inventory directs the implementer to read files
for "type information" and "pattern reference" using full-path pointers,
producing pure-read context bloat. Rig 2 had **49% of its read content
NEVER edited** (~56K tokens of waste).

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 3 | **Inline type signatures in the inventory** | Replace "see `types.ts:116` for `StandingOrder`" with the actual 200-char type def. | ~15-20% | small (sage-writer prompt) |
| 4 | **Inline pattern templates** | Replace "see `summon-relay.ts` as the template" with a 30-line pattern excerpt. | ~5-10% | small |
| 5 | **"Do not Read" markers on no-change files** | Files explicitly listed as unchanged still get pulled into context. Add explicit don't-read markers. | ~5% | trivial |
| 6 | **Strip vestigial pattern references** | Don't cite files that are about to be deleted or have been deprecated. | small | small |
| 7 | **Pre-quote source excerpts in spec** | Where the spec says "rewrite §11 of `petitioner-registration.md`", inline §11. | ~10% on doc-edit work | medium |

## 3. Implementer behavior — prompt-level nudges

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 8 | **Prefer Bash bulk edits for systematic renames** | Replace 10–20 sequential Edits with one `sed -i`. Saves turns AND tool-result accumulation. | ~5-10% | small (prompt) |
| 9 | **Targeted Reads (offset + limit)** | After a Grep returns line numbers, Read just the surrounding range. | ~5-15% | small |
| 10 | **Discourage repeat greps** | Same patterns re-searched 4+ times per session. Implementer should track its own search history. | ~2-5% | small (hard to enforce) |
| 11 | **Narrow test filters first, full suite only as final gate** | Implementer ran `pnpm -w test` 6+ times in rig 2. Should iterate with `--filter <pkg>`. | ~10% wall, ~5% cost | small |
| 12 | **Don't re-test packages you didn't change** | Avoid running tests on adjacent packages "just to be safe." | ~5% wall | small |

⚠ Idea #11 partially overlaps with the c-modz11rc finding (per-verify
output is only 0.9% of transcript). The refutation was about volume;
this is about wall time and turn count. They don't conflict.

## 4. Test infrastructure

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 13 | **Test impact analysis (turborepo / nx)** | Only run tests for changed packages. Saves wall time at every test invocation. | ~10-15% cost, ~50% wall time | medium (CI rework) |
| 14 | **Test design audit** | The 7213-second hung test in rig 2 was a defect. Sweep for tests with timer/sleep dependencies. | one-time outlier elimination | medium |

## 5. Engine pipeline decomposition

Per the X010 H4 simulation: a midpoint split with a 30K handoff saves
~33% median across 101 sessions (93% positive). Per-task splits are
worse than monolithic.

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 15 | **Checkpoint-and-fresh-session implement** (`c-modxxtu6` exists) | End implement after each manifest task with compact handoff. Targets the simulation's predicted ~50% savings. | ~30-50% on long sessions | large (architecture) |
| 16 | **Keep handoff under ~30K tokens** | Empirical savings vector is `(B − H)`. Bigger handoffs = smaller savings. | binding constraint on #15 | n/a |
| 17 | **Suppress per-session orientation tax** | Each fresh session must produce productive work in <5 turns; otherwise turn-count expansion eats savings (3.5× expansion was X010 H1 failure mode). | binding constraint on #15 | medium (handoff design) |
| 18 | **Subdivide implement into core + finalize** | Simpler split: "write code + green per-package tests" → handoff → "fix full-suite + update docs." | ~30-40% | medium |
| 19 | **Coarser checkpointing for small manifests** | Per-task checkpoint hurts when tasks <30 turns. Checkpoint every 2–3 tasks. | tunes #15 | small |

## 6. Subagent decomposition

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 20 | **Subagent for big-file analysis** | When implementer needs to "understand" a 50K file, dispatch a Task subagent that returns a 1K summary. | ~10-15% | small (prompt addition) |
| 21 | **Subagent for cross-package source** | "How does clockworks integrate?" → 1K integration brief, not 84K of source Reads. | ~10-15% | small |

## 7. Architectural patterns

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 22 | **Boundary-survey engine** | New astrolabe stage that runs after spec-writer, producing a focused integration brief for cross-package work. | ~10-15% | medium (new engine) |
| 23 | **Published types as integration contract** | Long-term: plugins consume framework via `.d.ts`, not source. Reduces grep surface; forces clean interfaces. | small immediate, large structural | large |

## 8. Repo structure

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 24 | **Polyrepo split** | Forces clean boundaries; enables per-repo CI scoping. Addresses symptoms more cheaply solvable with #13 + #22. | overlaps with #13 + #22 | very large |
| 25 | **Read-only sibling mounts** | If polyrepo: mount framework + plugin repos for grep. Smaller writable worktree, broader read surface. | n/a — coupled with #24 | medium |
| 26 | **`.agent-context/` per repo** | If polyrepo: per-repo curated context bundle (types, patterns, recent changes). | n/a — coupled with #24 | medium ongoing |

## 9. Upstream / Claude Code limitations

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 27 | **Context eviction primitive** | Currently Claude Code retains all tool results forever. A "drop this Read" affordance would cut carry-forward tax directly. | very large | not in our control |
| 28 | **Auto-compaction tuning** | Auto-compaction works gracefully. Could deliberately invoke it earlier as a poor man's checkpoint. | n/a — already happens | small (test) |

## 10. Operational

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 29 | **Rate-limit / rebase friction reduction** | Rig 2 lost ~4 hours to rate-limit pause + manual-merge spider. Independent of session economics. | varies, can be huge | medium |

---

## Compounding view

Stacked interventions on a rig-2-shape commission ($25.86 baseline):

| Stack | Savings | Resulting cost |
|---|---:|---:|
| Baseline | 0% | $25.86 |
| + Sonnet (#1) | ~75% | $6.50 |
| + Inventory excerpting (#3, #4, #7) | additional ~20% | $5.20 |
| + Test scoping (#13) | additional ~10% | $4.70 |
| + Handoff split (#15) on 150-turn equivalent | additional ~30% | $3.30 |

~8x cost reduction if all stack at expected rates. Even half that
compounding (4×) materially changes per-commission economics.

## Priority sequencing

| Priority | Theme | Includes | Why first |
|---:|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Inventory excerpting | #3, #4, #5, #7 | Pure prompt work in sage-writer/reader-analyst. No architecture change. Quick wins. ~20% on substantive work. |
| **P2** | Test scoping | #13 (and aux #14) | Saves wall time everywhere. Compounds with everything. Medium-effort infrastructure. |
| **P3** | Handoff split prototype | #15, #16, #17, #18, #19 | Simulation-validated ~30-50% savings on long sessions. Already exists as live click `c-modxxtu6`. Larger lift but the structural change with the most measured upside. |

**Sonnet test (#1)** sits aside as a cheap first probe — should run regardless of P1/P2/P3 sequencing. One quality-equivalence test on a representative commission, run both ways.

**Polyrepo (#24)** sits at the bottom of the list. The data says it
addresses symptoms cheaper interventions cover; it should be reached
for only after P1-P3 fail to deliver.

## Cross-references

- **X010 simulation artifact** (`experiments/X010-staged-sessions/artifacts/2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md`) — empirical basis for P3.
- **Rig analyses** (this conversation) — rig-1 vs rig-2 read-utilization
  finding is the empirical basis for the spec/inventory format category.
- **Existing intervention clicks under c-modxx4nj** — several ideas
  here overlap with existing siblings:
  - #15 = `c-modxxtu6` (checkpoint architecture, live)
  - #11 partly refuted by `c-modz11rc` (verify output volume measurement)
  - #19 related to `c-modxxyfz` (manifest body bound)
  - #20/#21 related to `c-modxxx26` (verify-reasoning subagent)
- **Cost-investigation case study** (`docs/case-studies/2026-04-25-implement-cost-investigation.md`)
  for the Apr-25 cliff findings that motivated the parent click.
