| 9 | **Targeted Reads (offset + limit)** | After a Grep returns line numbers, Read just the surrounding range. | ~5-15% | small |
| 10 | **Discourage repeat greps** | Same patterns re-searched 4+ times per session. Implementer should track its own search history. | ~2-5% | small (hard to enforce) |
| 11 | **Narrow test filters first, full suite only as final gate** | Implementer ran `pnpm -w test` 6+ times in rig 2. Should iterate with `--filter <pkg>`. | ~10% wall, ~5% cost | small |
| 12 | **Don't re-test packages you didn't change** | Avoid running tests on adjacent packages "just to be safe." | ~5% wall | small |

⚠ Idea #11 partially overlaps with the c-modz11rc finding (per-verify
output is only 0.9% of transcript). The refutation was about volume;
this is about wall time and turn count. They don't conflict.

## 4. Test infrastructure

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 13 | **Test impact analysis (turborepo / nx)** | Only run tests for changed packages. Saves wall time at every test invocation. | ~10-15% cost, ~50% wall time | medium (CI rework) |
| 14 | **Test design audit** | The 7213-second hung test in rig 2 was a defect. Sweep for tests with timer/sleep dependencies. | one-time outlier elimination | medium |

## 5. Engine pipeline decomposition

Per the X010 H4 simulation: a midpoint split with a 30K handoff saves
~33% median across 101 sessions (93% positive). Per-task splits are
worse than monolithic.

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 15 | **Checkpoint-and-fresh-session implement** (`c-modxxtu6` exists) | End implement after each manifest task with compact handoff. Targets the simulation's predicted ~50% savings. | ~30-50% on long sessions | large (architecture) |
| 16 | **Keep handoff under ~30K tokens** | Empirical savings vector is `(B − H)`. Bigger handoffs = smaller savings. | binding constraint on #15 | n/a |
| 17 | **Suppress per-session orientation tax** | Each fresh session must produce productive work in <5 turns; otherwise turn-count expansion eats savings (3.5× expansion was X010 H1 failure mode). | binding constraint on #15 | medium (handoff design) |
| 18 | **Subdivide implement into core + finalize** | Simpler split: "write code + green per-package tests" → handoff → "fix full-suite + update docs." | ~30-40% | medium |
| 19 | **Coarser checkpointing for small manifests** | Per-task checkpoint hurts when tasks <30 turns. Checkpoint every 2–3 tasks. | tunes #15 | small |

## 6. Subagent decomposition

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 20 | **Subagent for big-file analysis** | When implementer needs to "understand" a 50K file, dispatch a Task subagent that returns a 1K summary. | ~10-15% | small (prompt addition) |
| 21 | **Subagent for cross-package source** | "How does clockworks integrate?" → 1K integration brief, not 84K of source Reads. | ~10-15% | small |

## 7. Architectural patterns

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 22 | **Boundary-survey engine** | New astrolabe stage that runs after spec-writer, producing a focused integration brief for cross-package work. | ~10-15% | medium (new engine) |
| 23 | **Published types as integration contract** | Long-term: plugins consume framework via `.d.ts`, not source. Reduces grep surface; forces clean interfaces. | small immediate, large structural | large |

## 8. Repo structure

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 24 | **Polyrepo split** | Forces clean boundaries; enables per-repo CI scoping. Addresses symptoms more cheaply solvable with #13 + #22. | overlaps with #13 + #22 | very large |
| 25 | **Read-only sibling mounts** | If polyrepo: mount framework + plugin repos for grep. Smaller writable worktree, broader read surface. | n/a — coupled with #24 | medium |
| 26 | **`.agent-context/` per repo** | If polyrepo: per-repo curated context bundle (types, patterns, recent changes). | n/a — coupled with #24 | medium ongoing |

## 9. Upstream / Claude Code limitations

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 27 | **Context eviction primitive** | Currently Claude Code retains all tool results forever. A "drop this Read" affordance would cut carry-forward tax directly. | very large | not in our control |
| 28 | **Auto-compaction tuning** | Auto-compaction works gracefully. Could deliberately invoke it earlier as a poor man's checkpoint. | n/a — already happens | small (test) |

## 10. Operational

| # | Idea | What it changes | Est. savings | Effort |
|---:|---|---|---:|---|
| 29 | **Rate-limit / rebase friction reduction** | Rig 2 lost ~4 hours to rate-limit pause + manual-merge spider. Independent of session economics. | varies, can be huge | medium |

---

## Compounding view

Stacked interventions on a rig-2-shape commission ($25.86 baseline):

| Stack | Savings | Resulting cost |
|---|---:|---:|
| Baseline | 0% | $25.86 |
| + Sonnet (#1) | ~75% | $6.50 |
| + Inventory excerpting (#3, #4, #7) | additional ~20% | $5.20 |
| + Test scoping (#13) | additional ~10% | $4.70 |
| + Handoff split (#15) on 150-turn equivalent | additional ~30% | $3.30 |

~8x cost reduction if all stack at expected rates. Even half that
compounding (4×) materially changes per-commission economics.

## Priority sequencing

| Priority | Theme | Includes | Why first |
|---:|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Inventory excerpting | #3, #4, #5, #7 | Pure prompt work in sage-writer/reader-analyst. No architecture change. Quick wins. ~20% on substantive work. |
| **P2** | Test scoping | #13 (and aux #14) | Saves wall time everywhere. Compounds with everything. Medium-effort infrastructure. |
| **P3** | Handoff split prototype | #15, #16, #17, #18, #19 | Simulation-validated ~30-50% savings on long sessions. Already exists as live click `c-modxxtu6`. Larger lift but the structural change with the most measured upside. |

**Sonnet test (#1)** sits aside as a cheap first probe — should run regardless of P1/P2/P3 sequencing. One quality-equivalence test on a representative commission, run both ways.

**Polyrepo (#24)** sits at the bottom of the list. The data says it
addresses symptoms cheaper interventions cover; it should be reached
for only after P1-P3 fail to deliver.

## Cross-references

- **X010 simulation artifact** (`experiments/X010-staged-sessions/artifacts/2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md`) — empirical basis for P3.
- **Rig analyses** (this conversation) — rig-1 vs rig-2 read-utilization
  finding is the empirical basis for the spec/inventory format category.
- **Existing intervention clicks under c-modxx4nj** — several ideas
  here overlap with existing siblings:
  - #15 = `c-modxxtu6` (checkpoint architecture, live)
  - #11 partly refuted by `c-modz11rc` (verify output volume measurement)
  - #19 related to `c-modxxyfz` (manifest body bound)
  - #20/#21 related to `c-modxxx26` (verify-reasoning subagent)
- **Cost-investigation case study** (`docs/case-studies/2026-04-25-implement-cost-investigation.md`)
  for the Apr-25 cliff findings that motivated the parent click.

input.command:
SESSION_ID=fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
UMBRELLA=c-mok4nke6-06b21ff2a765

create_cat() {
  local goal="$1"
  nsg click create --parent-id "$UMBRELLA" --created-session-id "$SESSION_ID" --goal "$goal" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id'
}

CAT1=$(create_cat "Category 1 — Model selection. The single largest cost dial: Opus implement sessions are dominated by output tokens (Opus output is ~50× cache-read price). Most implement work is mechanical edits where Sonnet would suffice. Two ideas in this category: blanket Sonnet swap, and complexity-routed selection.")
echo "CAT1=$CAT1"
CAT2=$(create_cat "Category 2 — Spec and inventory format (NEW Apr 29). The reader-analyst inventory directs the implementer to read files for type info and pattern reference using full-path pointers, producing pure-read context bloat. Empirical evidence: rig 2 had 49% of read content NEVER edited (~56K tokens of waste, ~20% of cumulative cache reads). Rig 1 (mechanical) had 1.9% pure-reads. Five ideas here: inline type sigs, inline pattern templates, do-not-read markers, vestigial reference cleanup, pre-quote source excerpts.")
echo "CAT2=$CAT2"
CAT3=$(create_cat "Category 3 — Implementer behavior (prompt-level nudges). Five ideas at the implementer-prompt level: prefer Bash bulk edits, targeted Reads after Grep, discourage repeat-greps, narrow test filters first, don't re-test unchanged packages. Each is small effort with modest savings; bundled together they're worth pulling along with whichever sage-side change ships first. Overlaps partly with concluded c-modz11rc (verify volume refuted as cost lever) but on different axes (wall time, turn count) that aren't refuted.")
echo "CAT3=$CAT3"
CAT4=$(create_cat "Category 4 — Test infrastructure. Two ideas: install turborepo/nx for test impact analysis (only run tests for changed packages — saves wall time at every test invocation, including the 6+ in-session test runs we observed in rig 2), and audit test design for hung/timer-dependent tests (the 7213-second hang in rig 2 was a defect, not scale). Medium effort, compounds with everything else.")
echo "CAT4=$CAT4"
CAT5=$(create_cat "Category 5 — Engine pipeline decomposition. Five ideas centered on the X010 H4 simulation finding: a midpoint split with 30K handoff saves ~33% median across 101 sessions (93% positive). Per-task splits (X010 H1 piece-session) were 2.6x worse than monolithic because each piece paid orientation tax. Includes the existing c-modxxtu6 (checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture) plus implement-core/implement-finalize subdivision, handoff-size cap, orientation-tax suppression, and coarser-checkpointing tuning.")
echo "CAT5=$CAT5"
CAT6=$(create_cat "Category 6 — Subagent decomposition. Two ideas using the Task subagent affordance to keep big content out of the parent context: subagent for big-file analysis (50K file → 1K summary), and subagent for cross-package source reading (clockworks integration → 1K integration brief). Largely subsumed by inline excerpting in spec when that works, but a useful escape hatch when the spec didn't anticipate the question.")
echo "CAT6=$CAT6"
CAT7=$(create_cat "Category 7 — Architectural patterns. Two ideas: a new boundary-survey engine that produces a focused integration brief for cross-package work (medium lift, ~10-15% on cross-package commissions), and the long-term move toward published types as the integration contract (plugins consume framework via .d.ts not source — reduces grep surface, forces clean interfaces).")
echo "CAT7=$CAT7"
CAT8=$(create_cat "Category 8 — Repo structure. The polyrepo question that started the conversation. Three ideas: split into multiple repos (overlaps with #13 + #22 — addresses symptoms more cheaply solvable elsewhere), read-only sibling mounts to preserve grep surface, and per-repo .agent-context bundles. Sits at the bottom of the priority list because the data says cheaper interventions get most of the win without restructuring repos.")
echo "CAT8=$CAT8"
CAT9=$(create_cat "Category 9 — Upstream / Claude Code limitations. Two ideas not fully in our control: a context-eviction primitive (drop this tool result from context when no longer needed — would cut carry-forward tax directly; not in Claude Code today), and earlier auto-compaction invocation as a poor man's checkpoint (the 2026-04-16 monolithic baseline showed auto-compaction works gracefully).")
echo "CAT9=$CAT9"
CAT10=$(create_cat "Category 10 — Operational. One idea: rate-limit and rebase-friction reduction. Rig 2 lost ~4 hours to a rate-limit pause and a manual-merge spider for rebase conflict resolution — independent of all session-economics interventions. Worth a separate inquiry.")
echo "CAT10=$CAT10"

input.command:
SESSION_ID=fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
CAT1=c-mok4obzk-65bca0264e5f
CAT2=c-mok4ocec-d931228c268b
CAT3=c-mok4oct1-553c312ebe55
CAT4=c-mok4od7p-7d928f9db0f3
CAT5=c-mok4odme-44011f826fea
CAT6=c-mok4oe10-3eab3913cbb2
CAT7=c-mok4oefg-9f1cd2f945a3
CAT8=c-mok4oeu2-75eab8737d0e
CAT9=c-mok4of8r-2f5b238c1c61
CAT10=c-mok4ofnb-05bff4770e71

create_idea() {
  local parent="$1"
  local goal="$2"
  nsg click create --parent-id "$parent" --created-session-id "$SESSION_ID" --goal "$goal" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id'
}

# Category 1: Model selection
I1=$(create_idea "$CAT1" "Idea #1 — Switch implementer from Opus to Sonnet. Output tokens dominate Opus implementer cost (output is \$75/MTok vs cache-read \$1.50/MTok); most implement turns are mechanical edits that don't need Opus reasoning headroom. Estimated savings: ~70-80% on implement cost. Effort: trivial (config swap). Reversible. Run a quality-equivalence test on a representative commission first; commit if quality holds.")
I2=$(create_idea "$CAT1" "Idea #2 — Route by task complexity. Small/mechanical commissions → Sonnet; architectural ones → Opus. Heuristic could be predicted-files-touched count, manifest task count, or spec_chars (already a known cost predictor at +0.74 Pearson). Effort: small. Builds on idea #1 — only worth doing once #1 is validated.")
echo "Cat1: $I1 $I2"

# Category 2: Spec/inventory format (5 ideas)
I3=$(create_idea "$CAT2" "Idea #3 — Inline type signatures in the inventory. Today the reader-analyst writes 'see clockworks/src/types.ts:116 for StandingOrder shape' — implementer Reads the full 27K-char file. Replace with the actual type def inline (200 chars). Empirically rig 2 read clockworks/types.ts (27K), reckoner/types.ts (28K), clockworks/relay.ts (8.6K) and never edited two of them. Estimated savings: ~15-20% on substantive code commissions. Effort: small (sage-writer prompt change).")
I4=$(create_idea "$CAT2" "Idea #4 — Inline pattern templates. Today the inventory says 'see summon-relay.ts as the template' — implementer Reads the full 23K-char file. Replace with a 30-line excerpt of the pattern shape with a note: 'apply to tick.ts identically.' Estimated savings: ~5-10%. Effort: small (sage-writer prompt change).")
I5=$(create_idea "$CAT2" "Idea #5 — Add 'Do not Read' markers on no-change files. Files explicitly listed as 'no changes expected' still get pulled into context by curious implementers (rig 2: index.ts, package.json, schedulers/always-approve.ts, reckoner.test.ts all read despite explicit no-change notes). Add an explicit 'Do not Read.' annotation. Estimated savings: ~5%. Effort: trivial.")
I6=$(create_idea "$CAT2" "Idea #6 — Strip vestigial pattern references. Rig 2's inventory cited vision-keeper/decline-relay.ts as a pattern even though that plugin was about to be deleted in rig 1. Reader-analyst should detect 'this file is up for deletion' (e.g. via a recent-deletions-pending signal in the workspace) and not cite it. Estimated savings: small but specific.")
I7=$(create_idea "$CAT2" "Idea #7 — Pre-quote source excerpts in spec where the spec touches specific sections. If spec says 'rewrite §11 of petitioner-registration.md', inline the §11 contents in the spec rather than make implementer Read the full 41K-char doc. Spec-writer already loaded the doc to plan the change; carrying the excerpt forward avoids a re-Read. Estimated savings: ~10% on doc-edit work. Effort: medium (sage-writer redesign).")
echo "Cat2: $I3 $I4 $I5 $I6 $I7"
# Stash for priority 1 linking
echo "$I3 $I4 $I5 $I7" > /tmp/p1_ideas

# Category 3: Implementer behavior (5 ideas)
I8=$(create_idea "$CAT3" "Idea #8 — Prefer Bash bulk edits for systematic renames. Replace 10-20 sequential Edit calls (e.g. renaming a fixture string in a test file) with one sed -i / find -exec. Empirically rig 1 made 10 separate Edits to reckoner.test.ts to change individual fixture strings (~700 chars edited total) — could have been one Bash command. Estimated savings: ~5-10% on rename-heavy work. Effort: small (implement prompt nudge).")
I9=$(create_idea "$CAT3" "Idea #9 — Targeted Reads (offset + limit) after Grep. After a Grep returns line numbers, the implementer should Read just the surrounding range (e.g. Read --offset 290 --limit 20) rather than the entire file. Implementer already used this pattern once in rig 1 with --offset 1 --limit 100 — should default to it. Estimated savings: ~5-15%. Effort: small.")
I10=$(create_idea "$CAT3" "Idea #10 — Discourage repeat greps. In rig 2, the same patterns ('handleWritsChange|runCatchUpScan|stacks.watch.*clerk.*writs') were searched 4+ times with each result added to context. Implementer prompt should encourage tracking searched patterns within a session. Estimated savings: ~2-5%. Effort: small but hard to enforce.")
I11=$(create_idea "$CAT3" "Idea #11 — Narrow test filters in implementer iteration; full suite only as final gate. In rig 2, the implementer ran 'pnpm -w test' (full workspace) 6+ times during a single session — ~12 minutes wall + full test output added to context per call. Should iterate with 'pnpm --filter <pkg> test' and run full only at end. NOTE: Partly overlaps with concluded c-modz11rc (verify volume refuted as cost lever — output is only 0.9% of transcript). The volume refutation stands; this is about wall time and turn count which weren't refuted. Estimated savings: ~10% wall, ~5% cost. Effort: small.")
I12=$(create_idea "$CAT3" "Idea #12 — Don't re-test packages you didn't change. Avoid running tests on adjacent packages 'just to be safe.' Estimated savings: ~5% wall time. Effort: small (implement prompt).")
echo "Cat3: $I8 $I9 $I10 $I11 $I12"

# Category 4: Test infrastructure (2 ideas)
I13=$(create_idea "$CAT4" "Idea #13 — Test impact analysis (turborepo / nx). Only run tests for changed packages and their dependents. Cuts the 2-min full 'pnpm -w test' to seconds for most commissions. Saves both wall time AND context bloat (smaller test output added to context). Compounds with idea #11 (the prompt-level nudge becomes automatic). Estimated savings: ~10-15% cost, ~50% wall time on substantive work. Effort: medium (CI rework).")
I14=$(create_idea "$CAT4" "Idea #14 — Test design audit. The 7213-second hung test in rig 2 review was a defect (tick-handler test with timer/sleep dependency that didn't terminate cleanly). One-time sweep for tests with timer/sleep dependencies, hung-on-real-IO patterns, or unreasonable timeouts. Eliminates outlier costs that dominate when they fire. Effort: medium.")
echo "Cat4: $I13 $I14"
echo "$I13" > /tmp/p2_ideas

# Category 5: Engine pipeline decomposition (5 ideas)
# #15 already exists as c-modxxtu6 — link rather than duplicate
echo "Cat5 #15 — using existing c-modxxtu6"
I16=$(create_idea "$CAT5" "Idea #16 — Keep handoff under ~30K tokens. Empirical savings vector from the 2026-04-29 simulation: savings = (N-K) × (cr[K] - H) where H is handoff size. Bigger handoffs = smaller savings. 30K-handoff midpoint splits saved median 33% across 101 sessions; 100K handoffs save closer to 15%. Binding constraint on idea #15 (c-modxxtu6).")
I17=$(create_idea "$CAT5" "Idea #17 — Suppress per-session orientation tax. The X010 H1 piece-session experiment failed because each fresh session paid its own re-read-spec / re-grep-codebase / re-validate-prior-commits / re-state-the-plan tax — total turn count expanded 3.5x. A handoff architecture wins only if total turn count stays under ~1.3x monolithic. Each post-handoff session must produce productive work in <5 turns. Binding constraint on idea #15.")
I18=$(create_idea "$CAT5" "Idea #18 — Subdivide implement into core + finalize (simpler 2-split variant of #15). Cut implement into 'write code + green per-package tests' → 30K handoff → 'fix full-suite + update docs.' Two sessions, one handoff. Lighter lift than per-task checkpointing. Estimated savings: ~30-40% on substantive commissions. Effort: medium.")
I19=$(create_idea "$CAT5" "Idea #19 — Coarser checkpointing for small manifests. Per-task checkpoint hurts when tasks are <30 turns each (below the empirical naive break-even). Manifests with 5+ small tasks should checkpoint every 2-3 tasks instead of every task. Tunes idea #15 (c-modxxtu6) to small-commission regime. Effort: small. Related to existing c-modxxyfz (manifest body bound).")
echo "Cat5: $I16 $I17 $I18 $I19"

# Category 6: Subagent decomposition (2 ideas)
I20=$(create_idea "$CAT6" "Idea #20 — Subagent for big-file analysis. When implementer needs to 'understand' a 50K-char file (e.g. clockworks.ts to know how relays register), dispatch a Task subagent that reads it and returns a 1K summary. Big content stays in subagent context, not parent. Estimated savings: ~10-15% on substantive work. Effort: small (implement prompt addition). Related to existing c-modxxx26 (verify-reasoning subagent — same affordance, different application).")
I21=$(create_idea "$CAT6" "Idea #21 — Subagent for cross-package source. 'How does clockworks integrate with this?' becomes a 1K integration brief produced by a subagent, not 84K of cross-package source Reads in the parent. Largely subsumed by idea #4 (inline pattern templates) when the spec anticipates the question; useful escape hatch when it doesn't.")
echo "Cat6: $I20 $I21"

# Category 7: Architectural patterns (2 ideas)
I22=$(create_idea "$CAT7" "Idea #22 — Boundary-survey engine. New astrolabe stage that runs after spec-writer for cross-package commissions, producing a focused integration brief (5K instead of 84K of foreign source). Costs one small session, saves 20K+ tokens of permanent context downstream. Estimated savings: ~10-15% on cross-package work. Effort: medium (new engine). Compatible with idea #21 as the framework-level version of the same pattern.")
I23=$(create_idea "$CAT7" "Idea #23 — Published types as integration contract. Long-term: plugins consume framework via .d.ts not source. Reduces grep surface, forces clean interfaces, prerequisite for any polyrepo move (#24). Estimated savings: small immediate, large structural. Effort: large (semver discipline + tooling).")
echo "Cat7: $I22 $I23"

# Category 8: Repo structure (3 ideas)
I24=$(create_idea "$CAT8" "Idea #24 — Polyrepo split. Splitting nexus into framework / plugins / etc. Forces clean boundaries; enables per-repo CI scoping; smaller worktrees. BUT: addresses symptoms more cheaply solvable with idea #13 (test scoping) + idea #22 (boundary-survey). Polyrepo enforces structurally what discipline can do at lower cost. Estimated savings: overlaps with #13 + #22, marginal additional. Effort: very large (split + tooling + version coordination).")
I25=$(create_idea "$CAT8" "Idea #25 — Read-only sibling mounts. If we go polyrepo: mount framework + plugin repos into devcontainer for read-only grep access. Smaller writable worktree, broader read surface. Coupled with idea #24.")
I26=$(create_idea "$CAT8" "Idea #26 — Per-repo .agent-context/ bundle. If we go polyrepo: each repo ships a curated context bundle (types cheat sheet, event schema, idiomatic skeleton, recent-changes pointer). Coupled with idea #24. Maintenance burden — another thing to drift.")
echo "Cat8: $I24 $I25 $I26"

# Category 9: Upstream / Claude Code (2 ideas)
I27=$(create_idea "$CAT9" "Idea #27 — Context eviction primitive. Currently Claude Code retains all tool results forever — once a Read result is in context, it's there for the rest of the session even after the file has been edited. A 'drop this Read result, I'm done with it' affordance would cut carry-forward tax directly. Very large savings if available. Not in our control — upstream feature request to Anthropic / Claude Code.")
I28=$(create_idea "$CAT9" "Idea #28 — Auto-compaction tuning as poor-man's checkpoint. The 2026-04-16 monolithic baseline showed auto-compaction works gracefully (one fired at turn 11, one at turn 63, no quality cost observed). Could deliberately invoke it earlier to cap context size at a chosen threshold. Effort: small (test). Already partially happening — quantify the trigger threshold and consider deliberate invocation.")
echo "Cat9: $I27 $I28"

# Category 10: Operational (1 idea)
I29=$(create_idea "$CAT10" "Idea #29 — Rate-limit and rebase-friction reduction. Rig 2 lost ~4 hours to operational friction: ~2 hours rate-limit pause on the Anima provider, ~18 minutes manual-merge spider on rebase conflict, plus ~2 hours of test wall time waiting on the failing reckoner-tick test. Independent of all session-economics interventions. Worth a separate inquiry into rate-limit handling, rebase-conflict avoidance, and concurrent-seal contention.")
echo "Cat10: $I29"

# Save key IDs for priority links
echo "I15_EXISTING=c-modxxtu6" >> /tmp/p3_ideas
echo "$I16 $I17 $I18 $I19" >> /tmp/p3_ideas

input.command:
SESSION_ID=fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
UMBRELLA=c-mok4nke6-06b21ff2a765

P1=$(nsg click create --parent-id "$UMBRELLA" --created-session-id "$SESSION_ID" \
  --goal "Priority 1 — Inventory excerpting. Reformat the reader-analyst's inventory output so it inlines small excerpts (type signatures, pattern templates, source §-section quotes) rather than directing the implementer to Read entire files. Pure prompt-engineering work in sage-writer / reader-analyst — no architecture changes, no engine restructure, fully reversible. Bundles ideas #3 (inline type sigs), #4 (inline pattern templates), #5 (do-not-read markers), #7 (pre-quote source excerpts). Estimated stack savings: ~20% on substantive code commissions. Effort: small to medium (prompt rewrites + a measurement test). Why first: lowest-effort intervention with measured impact. Empirical motivation: rig 2 had 49% of read content NEVER edited (~56K tokens of waste, ~20% of cumulative cache reads). Acceptance signal: rerun a similar substantive commission post-change; pure-read share drops to <10%." \
  2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id')
echo "P1=$P1"

P2=$(nsg click create --parent-id "$UMBRELLA" --created-session-id "$SESSION_ID" \
  --goal "Priority 2 — Test scoping. Install turborepo (or nx) and configure per-package test scoping so the implement engine and review engine only run tests for changed packages and their dependents. Bundles idea #13 (test impact analysis), with idea #14 (test design audit) as an aux follow-up. Estimated savings: ~10-15% cost, ~50% wall time. Effort: medium (CI rework). Why second: compounds with everything else (every test invocation gets cheaper); also catches the in-implementer iteration cost (rig 2 ran 'pnpm -w test' 6+ times in-session = ~12 min wall + full test output added to context per call). Note: distinct from concluded c-modz11rc which refuted per-verify-output as a cost lever — that was about volume; this is about wall time and turn count which weren't refuted. Acceptance signal: implement-engine test wall time drops by ~5x on substantive commissions; full-suite output rarely appears in implementer context." \
  2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id')
echo "P2=$P2"

P3=$(nsg click create --parent-id "$UMBRELLA" --created-session-id "$SESSION_ID" \
  --goal "Priority 3 — Handoff split prototype. Implement the checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture (existing c-modxxtu6) for the implement engine: end the session at a chosen boundary, hand off via a compact (<30K-token) state summary, start a fresh session that picks up productive work in <5 turns. Empirical target: 30K-handoff midpoint splits saved median 33% across 101 sessions in the 2026-04-29 simulation (93% positive). Bundles idea #15 (= c-modxxtu6, existing live click), with binding constraints from ideas #16 (handoff-size cap) and #17 (orientation-tax suppression), and tuning variants #18 (core+finalize 2-split) and #19 (coarser checkpointing for small manifests). Effort: large (architecture). Why third: simulation-validated savings, but bigger lift than P1/P2 and benefits from running them first to measure residual cost surface before committing to architectural change. Acceptance signal: 2-session handoff implementation actually saves 25-50% cost vs monolithic on a same-shape commission run both ways; turn-count expansion stays under 1.3x." \
  2>/dev/null | jq -r '.id')
echo "P3=$P3"

content.content:
# c-mok4nke6-06b21ff2a765 [live]
> Apr 29 cost-optimization landscape — 29 interventions across 10 categories, drawn from a session-economics analysis of two recent rigs (rig-moji64hs, rig-moj12h4o) and 101 archived implement transcripts. Surfaces three priority bundles (P1 inventory excerpting, P2 test scoping, P3 handoff split prototype) plus the broader catalog of interventions ranging from trivial prompt nudges to repo-restructure. See .scratch/cost-optimization-landscape.md (becoming docs/ when finalized) for the full catalog. Compounding model suggests ~8x cost reduction if stack lands at expected rates; ~4x is realistic.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:05:27.678Z

## c-mok4obzk-65bca0264e5f [live]
> Category 1 — Model selection. The single largest cost dial: Opus implement sessions are dominated by output tokens (Opus output is ~50× cache-read price). Most implement work is mechanical edits where Sonnet would suffice. Two ideas in this category: blanket Sonnet swap, and complexity-routed selection.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:06:03.440Z

### c-mok4qazp-823b98abc8f3 [live]
> Idea #1 — Switch implementer from Opus to Sonnet. Output tokens dominate Opus implementer cost (output is $75/MTok vs cache-read $1.50/MTok); most implement turns are mechanical edits that don't need Opus reasoning headroom. Estimated savings: ~70-80% on implement cost. Effort: trivial (config swap). Reversible. Run a quality-equivalence test on a representative commission first; commit if quality holds.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:35.461Z

### c-mok4qbee-397d7220e96b [live]
> Idea #2 — Route by task complexity. Small/mechanical commissions → Sonnet; architectural ones → Opus. Heuristic could be predicted-files-touched count, manifest task count, or spec_chars (already a known cost predictor at +0.74 Pearson). Effort: small. Builds on idea #1 — only worth doing once #1 is validated.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:35.990Z

## c-mok4ocec-d931228c268b [live]
> Category 2 — Spec and inventory format (NEW Apr 29). The reader-analyst inventory directs the implementer to read files for type info and pattern reference using full-path pointers, producing pure-read context bloat. Empirical evidence: rig 2 had 49% of read content NEVER edited (~56K tokens of waste, ~20% of cumulative cache reads). Rig 1 (mechanical) had 1.9% pure-reads. Five ideas here: inline type sigs, inline pattern templates, do-not-read markers, vestigial reference cleanup, pre-quote source excerpts.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:06:03.972Z

### c-mok4qbtb-bd1b92930401 [live]
> Idea #3 — Inline type signatures in the inventory. Today the reader-analyst writes 'see clockworks/src/types.ts:116 for StandingOrder shape' — implementer Reads the full 27K-char file. Replace with the actual type def inline (200 chars). Empirically rig 2 read clockworks/types.ts (27K), reckoner/types.ts (28K), clockworks/relay.ts (8.6K) and never edited two of them. Estimated savings: ~15-20% on substantive code commissions. Effort: small (sage-writer prompt change).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:36.527Z

### c-mok4qc82-ba31acf38f95 [live]
> Idea #4 — Inline pattern templates. Today the inventory says 'see summon-relay.ts as the template' — implementer Reads the full 23K-char file. Replace with a 30-line excerpt of the pattern shape with a note: 'apply to tick.ts identically.' Estimated savings: ~5-10%. Effort: small (sage-writer prompt change).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:37.058Z

### c-mok4qcmz-2d965e778de4 [live]
> Idea #5 — Add 'Do not Read' markers on no-change files. Files explicitly listed as 'no changes expected' still get pulled into context by curious implementers (rig 2: index.ts, package.json, schedulers/always-approve.ts, reckoner.test.ts all read despite explicit no-change notes). Add an explicit 'Do not Read.' annotation. Estimated savings: ~5%. Effort: trivial.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:37.595Z

### c-mok4qd2a-834929a3381b [live]
> Idea #6 — Strip vestigial pattern references. Rig 2's inventory cited vision-keeper/decline-relay.ts as a pattern even though that plugin was about to be deleted in rig 1. Reader-analyst should detect 'this file is up for deletion' (e.g. via a recent-deletions-pending signal in the workspace) and not cite it. Estimated savings: small but specific.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:38.146Z

### c-mok4qdh4-fe3a9664c00a [live]
> Idea #7 — Pre-quote source excerpts in spec where the spec touches specific sections. If spec says 'rewrite §11 of petitioner-registration.md', inline the §11 contents in the spec rather than make implementer Read the full 41K-char doc. Spec-writer already loaded the doc to plan the change; carrying the excerpt forward avoids a re-Read. Estimated savings: ~10% on doc-edit work. Effort: medium (sage-writer redesign).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:38.680Z

## c-mok4oct1-553c312ebe55 [live]
> Category 3 — Implementer behavior (prompt-level nudges). Five ideas at the implementer-prompt level: prefer Bash bulk edits, targeted Reads after Grep, discourage repeat-greps, narrow test filters first, don't re-test unchanged packages. Each is small effort with modest savings; bundled together they're worth pulling along with whichever sage-side change ships first. Overlaps partly with concluded c-modz11rc (verify volume refuted as cost lever) but on different axes (wall time, turn count) that aren't refuted.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:06:04.501Z

### c-mok4qdw3-f5ed9358d0a2 [live]
> Idea #8 — Prefer Bash bulk edits for systematic renames. Replace 10-20 sequential Edit calls (e.g. renaming a fixture string in a test file) with one sed -i / find -exec. Empirically rig 1 made 10 separate Edits to reckoner.test.ts to change individual fixture strings (~700 chars edited total) — could have been one Bash command. Estimated savings: ~5-10% on rename-heavy work. Effort: small (implement prompt nudge).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:39.219Z

### c-mok4qebf-4421769b6709 [live]
> Idea #9 — Targeted Reads (offset + limit) after Grep. After a Grep returns line numbers, the implementer should Read just the surrounding range (e.g. Read --offset 290 --limit 20) rather than the entire file. Implementer already used this pattern once in rig 1 with --offset 1 --limit 100 — should default to it. Estimated savings: ~5-15%. Effort: small.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:39.771Z

### c-mok4qeqg-f2b9e9485ab2 [live]
> Idea #10 — Discourage repeat greps. In rig 2, the same patterns ('handleWritsChange|runCatchUpScan|stacks.watch.*clerk.*writs') were searched 4+ times with each result added to context. Implementer prompt should encourage tracking searched patterns within a session. Estimated savings: ~2-5%. Effort: small but hard to enforce.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:40.312Z

### c-mok4qf57-508718c42553 [live]
> Idea #11 — Narrow test filters in implementer iteration; full suite only as final gate. In rig 2, the implementer ran 'pnpm -w test' (full workspace) 6+ times during a single session — ~12 minutes wall + full test output added to context per call. Should iterate with 'pnpm --filter <pkg> test' and run full only at end. NOTE: Partly overlaps with concluded c-modz11rc (verify volume refuted as cost lever — output is only 0.9% of transcript). The volume refutation stands; this is about wall time and turn count which weren't refuted. Estimated savings: ~10% wall, ~5% cost. Effort: small.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:40.844Z

### c-mok4qfjz-7f295fee2a74 [live]
> Idea #12 — Don't re-test packages you didn't change. Avoid running tests on adjacent packages 'just to be safe.' Estimated savings: ~5% wall time. Effort: small (implement prompt).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:41.375Z

## c-mok4od7p-7d928f9db0f3 [live]
> Category 4 — Test infrastructure. Two ideas: install turborepo/nx for test impact analysis (only run tests for changed packages — saves wall time at every test invocation, including the 6+ in-session test runs we observed in rig 2), and audit test design for hung/timer-dependent tests (the 7213-second hang in rig 2 was a defect, not scale). Medium effort, compounds with everything else.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:06:05.029Z

### c-mok4qfyl-1b63dc13cf61 [live]
> Idea #13 — Test impact analysis (turborepo / nx). Only run tests for changed packages and their dependents. Cuts the 2-min full 'pnpm -w test' to seconds for most commissions. Saves both wall time AND context bloat (smaller test output added to context). Compounds with idea #11 (the prompt-level nudge becomes automatic). Estimated savings: ~10-15% cost, ~50% wall time on substantive work. Effort: medium (CI rework).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:41.901Z

### c-mok4qgd8-5ca59f5be553 [live]
> Idea #14 — Test design audit. The 7213-second hung test in rig 2 review was a defect (tick-handler test with timer/sleep dependency that didn't terminate cleanly). One-time sweep for tests with timer/sleep dependencies, hung-on-real-IO patterns, or unreasonable timeouts. Eliminates outlier costs that dominate when they fire. Effort: medium.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:42.428Z

## c-mok4odme-44011f826fea [live]
> Category 5 — Engine pipeline decomposition. Five ideas centered on the X010 H4 simulation finding: a midpoint split with 30K handoff saves ~33% median across 101 sessions (93% positive). Per-task splits (X010 H1 piece-session) were 2.6x worse than monolithic because each piece paid orientation tax. Includes the existing c-modxxtu6 (checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture) plus implement-core/implement-finalize subdivision, handoff-size cap, orientation-tax suppression, and coarser-checkpointing tuning.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:06:05.558Z

Files explicitly listed as 'no changes expected' still get pulled into context by curious implementers (rig 2: index.ts, package.json, schedulers/always-approve.ts, reckoner.test.ts all read despite explicit no-change notes). Add an explicit 'Do not Read.' annotation. Estimated savings: ~5%. Effort: trivial.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:37.595Z

### c-mok4qd2a-834929a3381b [live]
> Idea #6 — Strip vestigial pattern references. Rig 2's inventory cited vision-keeper/decline-relay.ts as a pattern even though that plugin was about to be deleted in rig 1. Reader-analyst should detect 'this file is up for deletion' (e.g. via a recent-deletions-pending signal in the workspace) and not cite it. Estimated savings: small but specific.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:38.146Z

### c-mok4qdh4-fe3a9664c00a [live]
> Idea #7 — Pre-quote source excerpts in spec where the spec touches specific sections. If spec says 'rewrite §11 of petitioner-registration.md', inline the §11 contents in the spec rather than make implementer Read the full 41K-char doc. Spec-writer already loaded the doc to plan the change; carrying the excerpt forward avoids a re-Read. Estimated savings: ~10% on doc-edit work. Effort: medium (sage-writer redesign).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:38.680Z

## c-mok4oct1-553c312ebe55 [live]
> Category 3 — Implementer behavior (prompt-level nudges). Five ideas at the implementer-prompt level: prefer Bash bulk edits, targeted Reads after Grep, discourage repeat-greps, narrow test filters first, don't re-test unchanged packages. Each is small effort with modest savings; bundled together they're worth pulling along with whichever sage-side change ships first. Overlaps partly with concluded c-modz11rc (verify volume refuted as cost lever) but on different axes (wall time, turn count) that aren't refuted.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:06:04.501Z

### c-mok4qdw3-f5ed9358d0a2 [live]
> Idea #8 — Prefer Bash bulk edits for systematic renames. Replace 10-20 sequential Edit calls (e.g. renaming a fixture string in a test file) with one sed -i / find -exec. Empirically rig 1 made 10 separate Edits to reckoner.test.ts to change individual fixture strings (~700 chars edited total) — could have been one Bash command. Estimated savings: ~5-10% on rename-heavy work. Effort: small (implement prompt nudge).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:39.219Z

### c-mok4qebf-4421769b6709 [live]
> Idea #9 — Targeted Reads (offset + limit) after Grep. After a Grep returns line numbers, the implementer should Read just the surrounding range (e.g. Read --offset 290 --limit 20) rather than the entire file. Implementer already used this pattern once in rig 1 with --offset 1 --limit 100 — should default to it. Estimated savings: ~5-15%. Effort: small.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:39.771Z

### c-mok4qeqg-f2b9e9485ab2 [live]
> Idea #10 — Discourage repeat greps. In rig 2, the same patterns ('handleWritsChange|runCatchUpScan|stacks.watch.*clerk.*writs') were searched 4+ times with each result added to context. Implementer prompt should encourage tracking searched patterns within a session. Estimated savings: ~2-5%. Effort: small but hard to enforce.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:40.312Z

### c-mok4qf57-508718c42553 [live]
> Idea #11 — Narrow test filters in implementer iteration; full suite only as final gate. In rig 2, the implementer ran 'pnpm -w test' (full workspace) 6+ times during a single session — ~12 minutes wall + full test output added to context per call. Should iterate with 'pnpm --filter <pkg> test' and run full only at end. NOTE: Partly overlaps with concluded c-modz11rc (verify volume refuted as cost lever — output is only 0.9% of transcript). The volume refutation stands; this is about wall time and turn count which weren't refuted. Estimated savings: ~10% wall, ~5% cost. Effort: small.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:40.844Z

### c-mok4qfjz-7f295fee2a74 [live]
> Idea #12 — Don't re-test packages you didn't change. Avoid running tests on adjacent packages 'just to be safe.' Estimated savings: ~5% wall time. Effort: small (implement prompt).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:41.375Z

## c-mok4od7p-7d928f9db0f3 [live]
> Category 4 — Test infrastructure. Two ideas: install turborepo/nx for test impact analysis (only run tests for changed packages — saves wall time at every test invocation, including the 6+ in-session test runs we observed in rig 2), and audit test design for hung/timer-dependent tests (the 7213-second hang in rig 2 was a defect, not scale). Medium effort, compounds with everything else.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:06:05.029Z

### c-mok4qfyl-1b63dc13cf61 [live]
> Idea #13 — Test impact analysis (turborepo / nx). Only run tests for changed packages and their dependents. Cuts the 2-min full 'pnpm -w test' to seconds for most commissions. Saves both wall time AND context bloat (smaller test output added to context). Compounds with idea #11 (the prompt-level nudge becomes automatic). Estimated savings: ~10-15% cost, ~50% wall time on substantive work. Effort: medium (CI rework).
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:41.901Z

### c-mok4qgd8-5ca59f5be553 [live]
> Idea #14 — Test design audit. The 7213-second hung test in rig 2 review was a defect (tick-handler test with timer/sleep dependency that didn't terminate cleanly). One-time sweep for tests with timer/sleep dependencies, hung-on-real-IO patterns, or unreasonable timeouts. Eliminates outlier costs that dominate when they fire. Effort: medium.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:07:42.428Z

## c-mok4odme-44011f826fea [live]
> Category 5 — Engine pipeline decomposition. Five ideas centered on the X010 H4 simulation finding: a midpoint split with 30K handoff saves ~33% median across 101 sessions (93% positive). Per-task splits (X010 H1 piece-session) were 2.6x worse than monolithic because each piece paid orientation tax. Includes the existing c-modxxtu6 (checkpoint-and-fresh-session architecture) plus implement-core/implement-finalize subdivision, handoff-size cap, orientation-tax suppression, and coarser-checkpointing tuning.
Status: live
Created by: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
Created: 2026-04-29T14:06:05.558Z

toolUseResult.content:
# Read Utilization Analysis — Implementer Pure-Read Bloat

**Date:** 2026-04-29
**Question:** Of the files the implementer Reads into context during a session,
which ones are actually edited or otherwise used to inform changes — and which
ones are pulled into context but never touched (pure context bloat)?

**Click:** Companion to the Apr 29 cost-optimization landscape under
`c-mok4nke6` — empirical motivation for Category 2 (Spec & inventory format)
and Priority 1 (Inventory excerpting).

## Method

For two recent implement transcripts, walk the tool call sequence:

1. Extract every `Read` call with file path and result size.
2. Extract every `Edit` and `Write` call, and any Bash call that modifies
   files (rm, mv, sed -i, git rm, etc.).
3. Classify each Read against subsequent modifications:
   - **Read AND edited** — file was Read, then later Edit/Written (legitimate work)
   - **Read AND bash-modified** — file was Read, then deleted/moved/sed-i'd via Bash
   - **Read but NEVER touched** — pure context bloat
4. Compute per-category breakdowns and total token weights.

Script: `scripts/h4_read_utilization.py`.

## Results

### Rig 1 — Vision-Keeper Cleanup (mechanical task)

| Category | Bytes | Files |
|---|---:|---:|
| Total Read content | 143,751 | 11 |
| Read AND edited | 140,997 | 10 |
| Read but NEVER touched | **2,754 (1.9%)** | **1** |

The single pure-read file was `docs/architecture/plugins.md` (Read twice
during exploration, never edited).

The implementer was efficient: it read what it needed to edit, with
minimal exploratory reads that didn't pay off in changes.

### Rig 2 — Reckoner Tick (substantive code change)

| Category | Bytes | Files |
|---|---:|---:|
| Total Read content | 458,640 | 21 |
| Read AND edited | 233,544 | 8 |
| Read but NEVER touched | **225,096 (49.1%)** | **13** |

Pure-read content was **~56K tokens** that sat in context for the rest
of the session. Across 147 turns of cumulative replay, this contributed
~8M cache reads — roughly **20% of the rig's total cache-read cost**.

Top pure-read files (read, never touched):

| File | Chars | Category |
|---|---:|---|
| `docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md` | 61,732 | doc |
| `clockworks/src/clockworks.ts` | 43,517 | source |
| `reckoner/src/reckoner.test.ts` | 33,684 | test |
| `clockworks/src/types.ts` | 26,952 | source |
| `clockworks/src/summon-relay.ts` | 23,021 | source |
| `reckoner/README.md` | 9,952 | doc |
| `clockworks/src/relay.ts` | 8,620 | source |
| `vision-keeper/src/decline-relay.ts` | 8,181 | source |
| (5 smaller files) | ~13K | mixed |

## The mechanism — astrolabe inventory format

Reading the inventory section of rig 2's plan (`nsg plan show
w-moiy8hkv`) reveals the source of the bloat:

The reader-analyst's inventory contains explicit sections directing the
implementer to read files for understanding, not for editing:

- **"Key types and interfaces (read-points, not copied verbatim)"** —
  lists files the implementer should consult for type definitions,
  citing path + line number rather than inlining the type:
  - `clockworks/src/relay.ts` for `RelayDefinition`, `RelayHandler`, etc.
  - `clockworks/src/types.ts` for `StandingOrder`, `ClockworksKit`
  - `reckoner/src/types.ts` for `Scheduler`, `SchedulerInput`, etc.

- **"Adjacent patterns"** — lists files the implementer should study as
  reference implementations:
  - `clockworks/src/summon-relay.ts` ("the template")
  - `vision-keeper/src/decline-relay.ts` ("much narrower")

- **"Affected files"** entries marked "no changes expected" still get
  Read by curious implementers:
  - `reckoner/src/index.ts` — "no changes expected"
  - `reckoner/src/reckoner.test.ts` — "(not opened) ... should pass unchanged"
  - `reckoner/src/schedulers/always-approve.ts` — "no changes"
  - `reckoner/package.json` — "no changes expected"

The implementer Read every single one. The inventory format communicates
"go look at this file" when what the implementer actually needs is "here's
the type signature" or "here's the pattern in 30 lines." Files are 10-100×
larger than their relevant excerpts.

A few additional observations:

- `vision-keeper/src/decline-relay.ts` was cited as a pattern even though
  vision-keeper was about to be deleted in the parallel rig 1 commission.
  Vestigial pattern reference — no detection mechanism caught it.
- `reckoner.test.ts` was Read (33K chars) despite being explicitly noted
  "(not opened) ... should pass unchanged." The implementer opened it
  anyway, presumably to confirm it didn't reference the deleted CDC code.

## Why rig 1 didn't suffer

Rig 1's task was mechanical: delete a plugin, rename strings, rewrite §11
of a doc. The inventory for that kind of task names files-to-modify, not
files-to-understand. Almost every Read mapped 1:1 to a subsequent Edit.

Substantive code changes (rig 2 type) are where the inventory pattern
bites. The agent legitimately needs to know how clockworks works to
register a tick relay correctly. The current inventory format conveys
that need by directing full-file Reads.

## Estimated savings if inventory was excerpted

For rig 2, replacing 56K tokens of pure-read content with ~5K of inline
excerpts:

- Final context: 375K → ~324K (drop ~51K)
- Average context per turn: 266K → ~230K
- Cumulative cache reads: 40.8M → ~33M
- Cost: $25.86 → ~$21 (saves ~$5, ~20%)

## Implications

1. **The inventory pattern is a structural cost driver for substantive
   commissions.** It doesn't show up in cleanup-style work.
2. **The fix is purely sage-side prompt work.** The reader-analyst /
   sage-writer should inline excerpts rather than emit pointers. No
   architecture changes, no engine restructure.
3. **This is a separate cost lever from H4 (handoff splitting).** They
   compound — H4 reduces carry-forward replay cost; inventory excerpting
   reduces what gets carried forward in the first place.

The Apr 29 cost-optimization landscape (`c-mok4nke6`) tags this as
**Priority 1 — Inventory excerpting**, bundling four ideas:

- Inline type signatures (idea #3, click `c-mok4qbtb`)
- Inline pattern templates (idea #4, click `c-mok4qc82`)
- "Do not Read" markers (idea #5, click `c-mok4qcmz`)
- Pre-quote source excerpts (idea #7, click `c-mok4qdh4`)

## Caveats

1. **N=2 sessions.** This finding is striking but the sample is tiny.
   Broadening to more substantive-code-change implements would confirm
   whether 49% pure-read share is typical or extreme.
2. **The classification doesn't account for "Read for context-setting
   that informed an edit but didn't directly map to one."** Some pure-reads
   may be legitimately useful — the agent reads X, doesn't edit X but
   makes a smarter edit to Y because of what it learned in X. We can't
   distinguish that from pure waste mechanically.
3. **Vestigial cleanup signal isn't free.** The reader-analyst would
   need access to a "files about to be deleted in concurrent commissions"
   index, which we don't have today.

## Data Sources

- Analysis script: `scripts/h4_read_utilization.py`
- Rig 1 transcript: see X010 simulation artifact (same path)
- Rig 2 transcript: see X010 simulation artifact (same path)
- Rig 2 inventory: `nsg plan show w-moiy8hkv-dfb884cac01b`

content.text: Let me also create an artifact for the read-utilization finding (since that's a separate empirical result that motivated category 2).

3. **Vestigial cleanup signal isn't free.** The reader-analyst would
   need access to a "files about to be deleted in concurrent commissions"
   index, which we don't have today.

## Data Sources

- Analysis script: `scripts/h4_read_utilization.py`
- Rig 1 transcript: see X010 simulation artifact (same path)
- Rig 2 transcript: see X010 simulation artifact (same path)
- Rig 2 inventory: `nsg plan show w-moiy8hkv-dfb884cac01b`

input.old_string:
- session: fbc9f9e3-a31c-4d6b-b833-a094f2778d97
  date: 2026-04-29
  item: "X010 H4 cost-model simulation. Conversation started open-ended on polyrepo (would splitting nexus help with commission cost/wall time?), and dug into two example rigs (rig-moji64hs vision-keeper cleanup, rig-moj12h4o reckoner tick) to characterize where the cost lives. Found implementer dominates cost (55-60% of rig spend) and is driven by cumulative cache-read replay across 130-150 turns × ~150-270K avg context. Derived a session-economics formula: total cost ≈ ∫context(t)dt; concave context curve makes cost super-linear in turn count (~40% above linear projection). Computed empirical break-even for naive splits — 30K-handoff midpoint splits save ~50% on 150-turn sessions vs ~10% with full re-read. Sean noted the prior X010 H1 piece-session experiment found splitting was way more expensive when each split re-orientation was forced. Built simulation script (h4a_naive_split.py + h4a_summary.py) testing both naive-split and handoff-split scenarios across 101 archived transcripts ≥ 50 turns plus the 2 recent rigs. Findings: naive split rarely pays off (73% of sessions never break even at any split point; empirical break-even at ~50-80 post-split turns, not 30 as the analytical formula suggested); 30K-handoff splits work in 93% of sessions with median 33% savings; the gap between naive and handoff is 30-95 percentage points. Wrote artifact 2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md formalizing the cost model and presenting cohort statistics. Drafted spec addendum in .scratch/x010-h4-addendum.md proposing H4a (naive break-even formula), H4b (handoff dominates), H4c (orientation-tax suppression as success criterion for c-modxxtu6) as sharpened sub-hypotheses with falsifiable thresholds. Spec edit pending Sean's review of the draft."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

Headline metrics across 433 decisions: override rate 3.5% (15/433; all `verdict: override`, 14 high-conf / 1 med / 0 low), low-confidence-confirm rate 25.4% (106/418 confirms). Distribution: confirm 418 (292 high / 20 med / 106 low), override 15 (14 high / 1 med / 0 low). Initial Coco answer drew from the wrong dataset (the patron-anima-gap one-shot snapshot, 14 writs / 167 decisions); Sean redirected with 'no, I mean it already does. so if you look at the last 25 writs… then query the plandocs (which have the same id) the data is in there' — recoded against the right source. Categorized all 106 low-conf-confirms across 13 buckets (top: docs/wording 27%, control flow / ordering 19%, naming/identifiers 16%) and surfaced the universal-rationale finding: 100% cite 'no principle speaks/fires — confirming the primer.' That reframes 'low confidence' from 'uncertain about the answer' to 'outside the principled review surface.' Drilled the 11 actual naming questions; 9/11 explicitly cite mirror-sibling-or-brief-verbatim as the rationale (planner is doing the right thing, principle just doesn't speak). Surfaced two reads (narrow-by-design vs grow-the-surface); Sean tabled. Parked click c-mok4c0wa (parent c-mod66upj) carrying the principle-surface-scope question.   item: "Patron-anima review stats across the last 25 completed mandate writs. Joined `nsg writ list --phase completed --type mandate --limit 25` with `nsg plan show <id>` to read each plandoc's per-decision `patron = { verdict, selection, confidence, rationale }` field — confirming the review fields are durably stamped on every plandoc, not retrospective. Headline metrics across 433 decisions: override rate 3.5% (15/433; all `verdict: override`, 14 high-conf / 1 med / 0 low), low-confidence-confirm rate 25.4% (106/418 confirms). Distribution: confirm 418 (292 high / 20 med / 106 low), override 15 (14 high / 1 med / 0 low). Initial Coco answer drew from the wrong dataset (the patron-anima-gap one-shot snapshot, 14 writs / 167 decisions); Sean redirected with 'no, I mean it already does. so if you look at the last 25 writs… then query the plandocs (which have the same id) the data is in there' — recoded against the right source. Categorized all 106 low-conf-confirms across 13 buckets (top: docs/wording 27%, control flow / ordering 19%, naming/identifiers 16%) and surfaced the universal-rationale finding: 100% cite 'no principle speaks/fires — confirming the primer.' That reframes 'low confidence' from 'uncertain about the answer' to 'outside the principled review surface.' Drilled the 11 actual naming questions; 9/11 explicitly cite mirror-sibling-or-brief-verbatim as the rationale (planner is doing the right thing, principle just doesn't speak). Surfaced two reads (narrow-by-design vs grow-the-surface); Sean tabled. Parked click c-mok4c0wa (parent c-mod66upj) carrying the principle-surface-scope question. Wrote categorized table to .scratch/low-conf-confirms.md (kept as scratch artifact). Found pre-existing YAML indent breakage in coco-log.yaml at line 1193 (col-0 dashes mixed with col-2 entries break parsing past that point); did not fix in-session — flagged as a future cleanup task."

  justification: sanctum

  date: 2026-04-29
  item: "Cost-optimization landscape — Apr 29 catalog and click-tree. Followup analysis on read utilization: extracted every file Read in the two rigs and classified by whether the file was subsequently edited. Stark asymmetry — rig 1 (mechanical cleanup) had 1.9% pure-read share; rig 2 (substantive Reckoner change) had 49.1% — ~56K tokens of context bloat from files Read for understanding but never modified. Traced the source to astrolabe's inventory format: 'Key types and interfaces (read-points)' and 'Adjacent patterns' sections direct the implementer to full-file Reads when 200-char inline excerpts would suffice. Consolidated 29 cost-reduction interventions identified across the conversation into a structured catalog (.scratch/cost-optimization-landscape.md) covering 10 categories: model selection, spec/inventory format, implementer behavior, test infrastructure, engine pipeline decomposition, subagent decomposition, architectural patterns, repo structure, upstream/Claude Code limits, operational. Built click-tree structure under c-modxx4nj (existing 'which intervention' parent): created umbrella c-mok4nke6 with 10 category clicks + 28 idea grandchildren + 3 priority clicks (P1 inventory excerpting c-mok4rf0h, P2 test scoping c-mok4rffg, P3 handoff split prototype c-mok4rfuk). Linked priorities to constituent ideas via 'related' link type. P3 includes the existing live click c-modxxtu6 (checkpoint architecture) plus four new constraint/variant clicks for handoff size, orientation-tax suppression, core+finalize subdivision, and coarser checkpointing. Compounding model: ~8x cost reduction if all stack at expected rates; ~4x realistic. Did NOT update X010 spec — that addendum still pending Sean's review of the .scratch/ draft."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

content.text:
Now the two remaining items: experiment updates and the birepo analysis.

## Experiment updates for your approval

These are the X010 / X011 / X015 changes the work suggests. Each is independently approvable.

### X010 — Staged Sessions (active)

**Update 1: Land the H4 cost-model addendum.**
Draft is in `.scratch/x010-h4-addendum.md`. Adds H4a/H4b/H4c as sharpened sub-hypotheses with falsifiable thresholds, plus a decision-framework table by session length. Backed by the `2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md` artifact.

**Update 2: Add the read-utilization finding as a parallel inquiry.**
The 49% pure-read share in rig 2 is a finding adjacent to (not the same as) H4. Two options:

- (a) Note it in the H4 addendum as motivation for inventory excerpting being a separate cost lever from handoff splitting.
- (b) Lift it into its own H5 hypothesis: *"Implementer cost is materially driven by inventory-induced pure-read context bloat — fixable without architectural change via prompt-format reform."* — falsifiable via a measured before/after on a substantive commission.

Recommend **(b)** if you want a clean experimental track for P1; (a) if P1 is just a tactical fix.

### X011 — Context Debt (currently `ready`, not yet active)

X011's research question is a near-perfect fit for the read-utilization work:
> *"How much context is consumed by tool output never referenced again, and can we reduce it?"*

**Proposed update: activate X011 with the Apr 29 read-utilization analysis as its first artifact.**
The methodology and dataset already exist. The script generalizes (point it at any transcript, get a pure-read share). Activating X011 lets us track this as a standing instrument across more rigs rather than as a one-off finding.

### X015 — Spec Detail as Model Substitute (currently `draft`)

X015 hypothesizes: *"Can planning-pipeline specs reduce the model capability threshold, enabling Sonnet-class models to match Opus outcomes?"*

**Proposed update: promote X015 to `ready` status and run the Sonnet-equivalence test described in idea #1 as its first phase.**
The cost-optimization work makes this experiment immediately actionable — and the inventory-excerpting work (P1) is plausibly the spec-detail mechanism X015 anticipates. Two adjacent investigations that could share a corpus.

### New experiment? (optional)

If you want to formalize P1 as its own experiment rather than fold into X010 H5: a small "Inventory Format Reform" experiment with a single hypothesis and pre-post measurement. I don't think it warrants a new X-number — it can ride X010 H5 or X011.

---

## Birepo analysis — sealed core + active plugins

The birepo framing is materially different from the full polyrepo question. Let me work through it on the data we have.

### What "core" and "plugins" probably mean

| Tier | Likely contents |
|---|---|
| **Core (sealed)** | `framework/core`, `framework/arbor`, `framework/cli`, plus stable plugins: `stacks`, `clerk`, `clockworks`, `codexes`, `tools`, `oculus`, `fabricator`, `loom` |
| **Plugins (active)** | `astrolabe`, `cartograph`, `ratchet`, `lattice`, `lattice-discord`, `animator`, `reckoner`, `sentinel`, `spider`, `parlour`, `clockworks-stacks-signals`, `claude-code`, `copilot` |

Roughly half-and-half by package count. Edge cases (clockworks-stacks-signals, claude-code) need explicit assignment.

### How birepo would change the cost surface

| Cost driver | Monorepo today | Birepo (plugins-active) |
|---|---|---|
| **Plugin commission test wall time** | Full `pnpm -r test` runs the world (~2 min, much more if a hung test fires) | Plugin tests + contract smoke test against pinned core (~30 sec) |
| **Plugin commission cache reads from core source** | Implementer freely Reads `clockworks.ts` (44K chars) | Depends on mount strategy — see below |
| **Cross-cutting changes** | One PR | Two PRs, version coordination, maybe a beta version of core |
| **Implementer worktree** | The whole monorepo | Just plugins, with core mounted |
| **Commission frequency** | All commissions hit same repo | Most commissions land in plugins-repo only |

The strongest cost case for birepo is the plugin test wall time. **But test impact analysis (idea #13) gives most of that benefit without the split.** The marginal value of birepo over `monorepo + turborepo` is enforcement of the boundary, not raw scoping.

### How birepo interacts with the Apr 29 cost findings

Going through each priority:

**P1 (inventory excerpting) — repo-agnostic.**
The reader-analyst's inventory format is the lever. Birepo doesn't change it; the same issue would manifest within plugins-repo for cross-plugin work (e.g., a cartograph commission that reads ratchet source). P1 should be done regardless.

**P2 (test scoping) — partially redundant with birepo.**
Birepo gives you per-repo CI naturally; turborepo gives you per-package CI within a monorepo. They're alternatives for the test-scoping benefit. **But turborepo also handles cross-repo-style scoping in the plugins-repo** (e.g., a cartograph change shouldn't need to re-test ratchet). So:
- If we go birepo: we still want turborepo *inside* plugins-repo
- If we stay monorepo: turborepo gets us most of the test-scoping benefit alone

**P3 (handoff split) — repo-agnostic.**
Cumulative-context-replay is a session-level cost, not a repo-level one. Birepo doesn't help.

### The cross-package source reading question (the rig 2 specific case)

Rig 2's implementer Read 84K chars of clockworks source (a "core" tier package) to understand how to register a new tick relay. In birepo:

- **Option A: clockworks source is mounted read-only.** Implementer can Read it the same way. **No cost change** — the inventory still points the implementer there, and reading 44K chars from a mount is the same as from in-tree.
- **Option B: clockworks is consumed via published `.d.ts` only.** Implementer can read types but not source. Cost goes down (smaller artifacts), BUT the implementer can't see the implementation — pattern reference reads (e.g., "see summon-relay.ts as the template") become broken. The boundary-survey engine (idea #22) becomes a hard prerequisite.

Option A is the easier transition; Option B forces the discipline but requires P1 + #22 + better published-types ergonomics first.

### Honest comparison: birepo vs monorepo + interventions

| Outcome dimension | Monorepo + P1+P2+P3 | Birepo (Option A) | Birepo (Option B) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plugin test wall time | ~5x faster (turborepo) | ~5x faster (per-repo CI) | ~5x faster |
| Pure-read context bloat | ~50% reduction (P1) | unchanged from monorepo | further reduction (forces P1) |
| Cumulative cache replay | ~33% reduction (P3) | unchanged | unchanged |
| Cross-cutting change cost | unchanged (one PR) | higher (two PRs, version pinning) | higher |
| Boundary discipline | informal | enforced | strongly enforced |
| Migration cost | low (CI tooling) | medium (split + dual CI + version pin) | high (medium + force published types) |
| Time to value | weeks | months | months |

### My read

Birepo is **more attractive than full polyrepo** because:

1. The split is at a meaningful boundary (sealed/active)
2. Most commission churn stays in one repo
3. Coordination overhead is bounded (2 repos, not N+1)
4. The cultural framing ("core is sealed") is genuinely useful regardless of repo structure

But it's still a heavy intervention compared to P1+P2+P3, which:

- Get most of the cost benefit
- Don't require version coordination
- Are reversible

**My recommendation:** treat birepo as a "phase 2" structural move, after P1+P2+P3 have shipped and the residual cost surface is measured. Specifically:

1. Ship P1+P2+P3 (or some subset). Measure new baseline cost.
2. Then evaluate: is core *actually* stable? (A "sealed" repo only works if the public surface is well-defined.) Is plugin commission frequency high enough that monorepo CI is the bottleneck?
3. If both yes → split. Otherwise → the cultural "core is sealed" discipline is achievable in monorepo via directory ownership rules + a published-types release process, without the split.

The cultural framing ("core is sealed") might be worth adopting **today** even within the monorepo — declaring certain packages as stable-API-locked and making changes to them require a higher bar of review. That's a cheap discipline change that gets you most of the boundary value without splitting anything.

### Specific question to answer before splitting

> **What fraction of recent commissions touched only "plugin" packages, vs touched both core and plugin?**

If 80%+ are plugin-only, birepo's friction tax is paid rarely and the split makes sense. If 50%+ are cross-cutting, the friction will dominate. We have the data to answer this — it's a query against the seal commits in `experiments/data/commissions/*/` cross-referenced with the proposed core/plugin partition. Worth doing before any split decision.

Want me to:

1. Move the X010 H4 spec addendum into `spec.md` (with whatever changes you call out from the four open questions in the draft)?
2. Activate X011 as a real experiment using the read-utilization work as its first artifact?
3. Promote X015 to `ready` status?
4. Pull the cross-cutting-commission stat I just described — to inform the birepo decision empirically?

Any combination is fine — just tell me which to proceed with.

attachment.snippet:
1	# X010 — H4 Addendum Draft (2026-04-29)
2	
3	This is a draft of the addendum to add to `experiments/X010-staged-sessions/spec.md`.
4	Once Sean approves, it lands at the bottom of the spec, after the "Addendum
5	(Apr 25, 2026) — Post-Manifest Regime Change" section. The new content carries
6	forward the cost-model formalization and three sharpened sub-hypotheses backed
7	by the 2026-04-29 simulation artifact.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Addendum (Apr 29, 2026) — H4 Cost Model & Sub-Hypotheses
12	
13	The Apr 25 addendum re-opened H4 with one candidate architecture
14	(`c-modxxtu6`, checkpoint-and-fresh-session at manifest-task boundaries) but
15	no quantitative target thresholds. A subsequent session-economics analysis
16	during a polyrepo design conversation derived a cost model for split-session
17	costs and ran it against the existing transcript archive (101 sessions ≥ 50
18	turns; see [`artifacts/2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md`](artifacts/2026-04-29-h4-naive-split-simulation.md)).
19	The findings sharpen H4 into three measurable sub-hypotheses.
20	
21	### Cost Model
22	
23	For a session with N turns and per-turn cache-read `cr[i]`:
24	
25	- **Monolithic cost** = `Σ cr[i]` (cumulative cache-read across all turns).
26	- **Handoff split at K with handoff size H** = `monolithic − (N−K) × (cr[K] − H)`.
27	  Savings are linear in remaining-turns × (context-at-split minus handoff).
28	- **Naive split at K** = `monolithic + baseline_cost − (N−K) × α`, where
29	  `α = cr[K] − B` is the phase-1-specific accumulated content and
30	  `baseline_cost` is the cost session 2 incurs to re-do the orientation reads.
31	
32	The model assumes per-turn cache-read dominates session cost (60–94% per the
33	2026-04-03 analysis). Output and cache-creation costs are not modeled —
34	giving a conservative lower bound on naive-split overhead.
35	
36	### H4a — Naive Splitting Has a Per-Half Break-Even
37	
38	Splitting a session into two without a structured handoff (each session does
39	its own baseline orientation) breaks even only when the post-split work has
40	enough turns to amortize the re-read tax: `T_post ≥ B_cost / α`.
41	
42	**Prediction:** for current-regime implement sessions (Opus 4, ~150 turns
43	total, baseline B ≈ 100–200K, α ≈ 50–100K), naive splitting requires roughly
44	**50–80 turns of post-split work** to break even. Below that, baseline
45	re-read cost exceeds the savings.
46	
47	**Empirical confirmation:** simulation across 101 transcripts shows
48	**73% have NO split point at which naive splitting saves money**, and the 27%
49	that do have median break-even at `T_post ≈ 79 turns`. Both recent rigs
50	(139 and 155 turns) fall into the "naive never wins" majority.
51	
52	**Falsification path:** if a sample of in-flight commissions shows naive
53	break-even consistently below 30 turns OR consistently above 120 turns, the
54	formula calibration is wrong and the model needs revision.
55	
56	### H4b — Handoff Size Determines Savings Magnitude
57	
58	The savings from splitting scale primarily with `(B − H)`, not with α. A
59	small handoff captures most of the available savings ceiling.
60	
61	**Prediction:**
62	
63	- 30K handoff → median ~33% savings on midpoint splits (range 26–42%).
64	- 60K handoff → median ~25% savings (slightly lower).
65	- 100K handoff → median ~15% savings.
66	- 0K handoff (full re-read / naive) → median *negative* savings.
67	
68	The asymmetry is dramatic: a 30K handoff saves ~3× more than naive splitting
69	even where naive can win at all.
70	
71	**Empirical confirmation:** at 30K handoff midpoint splits, **93% of 101
72	sessions show positive savings** with median 33%. Sessions with naive
73	break-even still see handoff capture 30–95 additional percentage points of
74	savings above what naive achieves.
75	
76	**Falsification path:** a live test with a real handoff implementation that
77	saves substantially less than the model predicts (e.g., <15% on a session
78	the model predicts 30%+) would invalidate the cost model — likely because of
79	unmodeled costs (output expansion, cache-creation taxes, orientation
80	turns at session 2's start).
81	
82	### H4c — Handoff Architecture Must Suppress Per-Session Orientation
83	
84	The 2026-04-16 H1 monolithic-baseline result (decomposed Rig 2 cost 2.6× the
85	monolithic equivalent) was driven primarily by **turn-count expansion**:
86	piece-session split work into 6 pieces averaging 44 turns each, but the
87	*total* turn count was 3.5× the monolithic version. Each piece paid its own
88	orientation tax (re-read spec, re-grep codebase, re-validate prior commits,
89	re-state the plan, draft its own commit message). The handoff candidate in
90	`c-modxxtu6` only succeeds if it suppresses that tax.
91	
92	**Prediction:** a handoff architecture wins only when total turn-count across
93	all sessions stays within ~1.3× monolithic. Above ~1.5× expansion, even
94	handoff savings are eaten by extra turns.
95	
96	**Empirical signal to watch:** in a live H4 test, measure the first 10 turns
97	of each post-handoff session. If they spend more than 5 turns orienting
98	(reading spec/files/git log/prior commits) before making productive edits,
99	the handoff is too thin or the prompt structure isn't using it.
100	
101	**Falsification path:** if a well-designed handoff architecture still
102	produces >1.3× turn expansion (no matter how the handoff is structured),
103	this hypothesis suggests the orientation tax is *intrinsic* to fresh
104	sessions and can't be avoided structurally — the answer would shift back
105	toward "make monolithic sessions cheaper" (e.g., context eviction in the
106	provider) rather than "split with handoffs."
107	
108	### Updated H4 Decision Framework
109	
110	Combining H4a–H4c, the staging-vs-monolithic decision becomes:
111	
112	| Session length | Monolithic? | 2-session naive? | 2-session handoff? | Per-task split? |
113	|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
114	| < 80 turns | ✓ default | ✗ never | (marginal) | ✗ never |
115	| 80–150 turns | ✓ default | ✗ usually loses | ✓ +30% median | ✗ never |
116	| 150–250 turns | (acceptable) | ~ often loses | ✓ +35% median | ✗ never |
117	| 250+ turns | (cost penalty) | ~ sometimes wins | ✓ +50% median | ~ depends on per-piece turns |
118	
119	Per-task split (what X010 H1 actually tested) remains a poor default —
120	sub-50-turn pieces don't clear naive break-even, and the orientation tax
121	compounds across many small sessions. **The architectural improvement
122	direction is fewer, larger splits with structured handoffs, not many small
123	splits.**
124	
125	### Implication for `c-modxxtu6`
126	
127	The candidate architecture (checkpoint after each manifest task) is on the
128	right track but needs to satisfy both:
129	
130	1. **Handoff stays under ~30K** — the contents must be a tight summary
131	   (commit SHAs, manifest progress, active-task pointer), not a transcript
132	   carry-forward.
133	2. **Each post-handoff session begins productive work within ~5 turns** —
134	   the handoff prompt structure must replace re-orientation, not invite it.
135	
136	If a manifest has many small tasks (5+ tasks averaging <30 turns each),
137	checkpointing per task may still hurt because of orientation tax across
138	many sessions. A coarser strategy (checkpoint every 2–3 tasks) likely
139	gives better economics than per-task.
140	
141	### Updated Procedure
142	
143	The X010 procedure (Phase 1–3) is updated to add a new phase before live
144	testing:
145	
146	#### Phase 1.5 — Cost-Model Validation (H4a, H4b)
147	
148	Already complete (2026-04-29 simulation artifact). Confirms the cost model
149	predictions against the existing transcript archive. No further work needed
150	unless the cost model is challenged.
151	
152	#### Phase 2 — Live H4 Test (newly framed)
153	
154	Replace the original Phase 2's monolithic-vs-staged comparison with a
155	narrower test: monolithic vs 2-session-handoff midpoint split, on the
156	*same* commission body, with a deliberate <30K handoff design.
157	
158	- Pick an in-flight commission of 100+ expected turns
159	- Run as monolithic, capture cost, turns, quality
